class_schedule.topic: The Communication Process, Audience Analysis, and Why Communication Matters
learning_objectives: Describe the transactional model of communication and identify every point where meaning can be distorted or lost
Conduct a structured audience analysis covering role, knowledge level, attitude, stakes, and medium preference
Explain with concrete examples how communication failures translate directly into business losses, legal liability, and damaged trust
Distinguish sender-centered from receiver-centered communication and rewrite a self-focused message into audience-focused language
readings: Guffey & Loewy, Essentials of Business Communication (12th ed.), Ch. 1 - Communicating in the Digital-Age Workplace
Locker & Kaczmarek, Business Communication: Building Critical Skills (8th ed.), Ch. 1-2
HBR Guide to Better Business Writing, Introduction and Ch. 1 - Know Your Audience
Shannon & Weaver, 'A Mathematical Theory of Communication' (1948) - foundational paper on noise and channel

lecture_notes.introduction: Every quarter, Warren Buffett sits down and writes the Berkshire Hathaway shareholder letter. He does not delegate it. He does not pad it with jargon. He imagines his sisters Doris and Bertie, who are intelligent but not finance professionals, and he writes as if he is explaining the year to them personally. The result is arguably the most-read piece of business writing in the world, and it has helped Berkshire attract long-term investors who stay through volatility other companies cannot survive. Contrast that with the 2010 Deepwater Horizon oil spill, where BP CEO Tony Hayward told reporters 'I would like my life back' while oil was still gushing into the Gulf of Mexico, eleven workers were dead, and fishing communities were being destroyed. That single sentence did not cause the spill, but it cost Hayward his job, cost BP billions in reputation damage, and became the textbook example of how one tone-deaf line can outweigh thousands of pages of technical response. Business communication is not a soft skill you pick up on the job. It is the operating system of every organization: the medium through which strategy becomes action, the mechanism by which trust is built or destroyed, and the instrument through which ideas either travel or die inside your own head. This course treats communication as a rigorous professional discipline with testable principles and measurable outcomes, beginning today with the deceptively simple question: who exactly am I writing to, and what do they need from me?
key_concepts: Transactional model of communication: sender encodes, channel transmits, receiver decodes, and feedback loops back; every stage introduces potential noise (semantic, physical, psychological, cultural) that distorts the intended meaning

Audience analysis framework: identify primary audience (decision maker), secondary audience (implementers and influencers), and gatekeepers (assistants, legal, compliance) who may block or shape the message before it reaches the primary audience

The 'what's in it for me' (WIIFM) principle: readers and listeners filter every message through self-interest; messages that do not answer WIIFM within the first few seconds are skimmed, ignored, or deleted

Sender-centered vs receiver-centered language: sender-centered ('We are pleased to announce our new policy') foregrounds the writer's actions and feelings; receiver-centered ('Starting Monday, you can submit expense reports from your phone') foregrounds what the reader will do and gain

Channel selection: richness spectrum from face-to-face (highest, carries tone and body language) through video, phone, synchronous chat, email, and formal document (lowest, but most permanent and searchable); match channel richness to message complexity and emotional weight

Noise types: semantic noise (jargon, ambiguity), physical noise (bad connection, noisy room), psychological noise (reader is stressed, distracted, biased), cultural noise (differing norms around directness, hierarchy, time)

Stakes analysis: low-stakes routine updates can be asynchronous and terse; high-stakes messages (layoffs, crisis response, strategic change) require synchronous channels, rehearsal, and multiple rounds of review

The cost of bad communication: a 2022 Grammarly/Harris poll estimated $1.2 trillion annually in US productivity lost to poor workplace communication; individual cases include United Airlines losing roughly $1 billion in market cap after Oscar Munoz's initial 'reaccommodate' statement about dragging Dr. David Dao off flight 3411

Feedback as verification, not permission: professional communicators check understanding ('Can you play back what you heard?') rather than asking 'Does that make sense?' which gets a reflexive yes even when nothing landed

Ethical responsibility of the sender: the burden of being understood rests with the communicator, not the audience; blaming a reader for 'not getting it' is a diagnostic of communicator failure, not audience failure
lecture_notes.demonstration: Take a real corporate email that was sent company-wide at a Fortune 500 firm: 'As part of our ongoing strategic realignment to drive synergies and unlock shareholder value, we are pleased to announce organizational changes effective immediately. Please review the attached deck for further details.' Break down what is wrong: sender-centered ('we are pleased'), euphemistic ('realignment'), vague ('changes'), demands effort ('review the attached deck'), and buries the lede. Rewrite it receiver-centered: 'Today we are eliminating the Product Marketing group and moving its work into Brand Marketing. If you are one of the 42 people affected, your manager will meet with you by 3pm today to explain next steps. Everyone else: here is what changes in your work starting Monday...' Discuss how the rewrite answers WIIFM in the first sentence and treats the reader as an adult who deserves the truth.
setup_requirements: Printed copy of a real Berkshire Hathaway shareholder letter (2020 or later) for close reading
Access to the United Airlines 3411 incident coverage including Oscar Munoz's three successive public statements
Audience analysis worksheet with role/knowledge/attitude/stakes/channel columns
A sample of three anonymized real corporate emails from the instructor's files for in-class rewriting

review.value: Before you type a single word, name your primary audience out loud by role and mental state. 'The VP of Operations, who is already behind on his Q4 budget and does not want another surprise' is a useful answer. 'Leadership' is not.

Layoffs over email are cruelty dressed as efficiency. Praise buried in a 40-page report is invisible. The Better.com Zoom layoff of 900 people by CEO Vishal Garg in December 2021 is the canonical case of a channel that was technically fast but humanly catastrophic.

Rewriting 'We are implementing a new expense system' into 'You can now submit receipts by photo from your phone, and reimbursements land in your account in 3 days instead of 14' produces measurably higher read rates and fewer follow-up questions, as demonstrated by internal A/B tests at companies like Atlassian and Basecamp.

In knowledge work, your output is almost entirely communication: emails, documents, presentations, meetings, code comments. If your communication is unclear, your work is effectively invisible even if the underlying thinking is brilliant. Clarity is not decoration; it is the deliverable.

class_schedule.topic: The Writing Process: Planning, Drafting, Revising, Editing
learning_objectives: Apply a disciplined four-stage writing process (plan, draft, revise, edit) and explain why collapsing these stages produces worse output in more time
Produce a one-page planning document before drafting any business message of consequence
Distinguish revising (structural changes to content and order) from editing (sentence-level polish) and apply each appropriately
Use reverse outlining to diagnose and fix structural problems in draft documents
readings: Guffey & Loewy, Essentials of Business Communication, Ch. 2-4 - The Writing Process
HBR Guide to Better Business Writing, Ch. 2-4 (Planning, Drafting, Revising)
Zinsser, On Writing Well (30th anniversary ed.), Ch. 1-4
Williams & Bizup, Style: Lessons in Clarity and Grace (12th ed.), Lessons 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Amateur writers sit down at a keyboard and start typing the opening sentence. Two hours later they have three paragraphs they are unhappy with, no clear ending, and a sinking feeling that the whole thing is wrong. Professional writers spend the first twenty minutes not writing at all. They ask what the document is supposed to accomplish, who will read it, what the reader already knows, what action they want the reader to take, and what objections the reader might raise. Only then do they begin to draft, and when they do, they draft fast and ugly, knowing that the first pass is raw material for revision, not a finished product. The four-stage writing process (plan, draft, revise, edit) is not a pedagogical convenience invented by textbook authors. It reflects how every professional writer, from Stephen King to Atul Gawande to the speechwriters in the West Wing of the White House, actually produces publishable work. When students collapse the stages, trying to plan, draft, and edit in one pass, they get stuck because they are asking their brain to do incompatible things simultaneously: generate freely and critique ruthlessly. Today we separate these stages into distinct activities, each with its own toolkit, and we practice the revision techniques that transform a first draft into a document a busy executive will actually read.
key_concepts: Planning stage: define purpose (inform, persuade, instruct, build relationship), identify primary and secondary audiences, list what the reader must know versus what you want to say, choose medium, and set a single measurable success criterion

The one-page plan: purpose statement in one sentence, audience description, three to five key points in priority order, the single call to action, and the anticipated objections with pre-drafted responses

Drafting stage: produce text without editing; turn off internal critic; write ugly, misspelled, incomplete prose if necessary just to get structure on the page; Anne Lamott's 'shitty first drafts' concept from Bird by Bird is the governing principle

Revising stage (macro): cut whole sections that do not serve the purpose, reorder for reader priority (most important first, not most chronologically recent first), rewrite weak openings, combine or split paragraphs for logical flow
Editing stage (micro): tighten sentences, replace weak verbs, cut filler ('in order to' becomes 'to', 'at this point in time' becomes 'now'), verify facts and numbers, proofread for typos

Reverse outlining: after drafting, write a one-line summary of each paragraph in the margin; if the summary reads as a coherent outline of the argument, structure is good; if it reads as a random list, the draft needs re-ordering

The bottom line up front (BLUF) principle: state your conclusion or recommendation in the first paragraph, then support it; this inverts the academic 'build to a conclusion' habit and matches how executives actually read

Pyramid Principle (Barbara Minto): structure documents as a governing thesis supported by three to seven independent, mutually exclusive reasons, each supported by evidence; used at McKinsey and every major consulting firm
The two-pass read-aloud: read your draft aloud once for flow (you will hear awkward sentences you cannot see), then once silently for accuracy and typos (you will see errors the ear misses)

Timeboxing each stage: if you have four hours for a document, spend one hour planning, ninety minutes drafting, sixty minutes revising, thirty minutes editing; students typically spend ten minutes planning and three hours in a drafting/editing loop that never terminates
lecture_notes.demonstration: Take a real student-written memo draft: three pages, no clear recommendation, buried lede, strong evidence mixed with filler. On screen, apply reverse outlining: write a one-sentence summary of each paragraph. Show that the summaries reveal the structural problem: paragraphs 1, 4, and 6 actually contain the argument, while 2, 3, 5, and 7 are background the reader does not need. Cut to the three essential paragraphs, reorder so the recommendation is paragraph 1, and rewrite the opening sentence from 'As you may be aware, the team has been evaluating several options over the past quarter' into 'Recommendation: adopt the Salesforce contract before the December 15 quote expires. Here are the three reasons and the two risks.' The revised memo is one page, starts with the point, and reads in under two minutes.
setup_requirements: Printed sample memo draft with intentional structural problems for in-class reverse outlining
Williams & Bizup Style exercises on nominalizations and weak verbs
One-page planning template (purpose, audience, points, call to action, objections)
Timer for enforcing stage time limits during in-class drafting exercise

review.value: If you are deciding what to say and how to say it at the same time, you are asking your brain to both generate and critique, which produces writer's block. Decide the content with the keyboard closed, then draft with the critic off.

Hemingway said 'the first draft of anything is shit.' He was right. Your job in drafting is to produce raw material; your job in revising is to throw away most of it. Students who try to write publishable prose on the first pass produce neither prose nor revision.

If you cannot write a one-sentence summary of each of your paragraphs, your reader cannot either. Reverse outlining is the fastest structural diagnostic in professional writing and should become automatic for any document longer than a page.

Executives read the first sentence and the last sentence; everything in between is optional. Bottom Line Up Front is not rude, it is honest. The academic habit of building to a conclusion wastes your reader's time and signals that you are unsure of your own point.

learning_objectives: Write professional emails that get read, understood, and acted on in under 30 seconds
Construct subject lines that convey purpose, urgency, and action without clickbait or vagueness
Apply the action-request-deadline (ARD) pattern to any email that needs the reader to do something
Distinguish when to use email, when to use chat, when to use a memo, and when to pick up the phone
readings: Guffey & Loewy, Essentials of Business Communication, Ch. 5-6 - Emails, Memos, and Routine Messages
HBR Guide to Better Business Writing, Ch. 7 - Emails That Get Results
Locker & Kaczmarek, Business Communication, Ch. 9 - Informative and Positive Messages
Cal Newport, A World Without Email (2021), Ch. 1-3 for context on email overload

lecture_notes.introduction: The average knowledge worker receives 121 emails per day and spends 28 percent of the workweek on email, according to a widely cited McKinsey Global Institute study. That means your email is competing with 120 others for the reader's finite attention, most of which will be processed in under 11 seconds each. The economics are brutal: if your subject line is vague, your email is deleted unread. If your opening sentence does not explain why the reader should care, your email is archived. If your request is buried in paragraph three, the reader will forget to act and blame you when the deadline is missed. This is not a failure of the reader; it is a failure of the writer to respect the cognitive load imposed on a busy human. Today we learn the craft of email as a professional medium: subject lines that are promises, openings that answer WIIFM, requests that are unambiguous, and sign-offs that close the loop cleanly. We also learn when not to send an email at all, because the single fastest way to improve your communication effectiveness is to replace a bad email with the right channel: a two-minute phone call, a five-minute Slack thread, or a fifteen-minute meeting.
key_concepts: Subject line as promise: 'Decision needed by Friday: choose vendor A or B for Q1 launch' tells the reader what the email is, what is required, and when; 'Quick question' and 'Touching base' are anti-patterns that train readers to ignore you
The ARD pattern: Action (what you need), Request (from whom specifically, not the vague 'the team'), Deadline (with a real date, not 'ASAP' or 'when you get a chance')

First-sentence test: the first sentence must state either the request, the key information, or the reason for writing; openings like 'Hope you are doing well' waste the reader's most valuable cognitive moment
Single-purpose emails: one email, one topic, one decision; bundling three unrelated items in one message guarantees at least one will be lost

The CC ladder: direct recipients are expected to act; CC'd people are informed; BCC is for mass announcements where you do not want to expose the list; overusing CC creates the 'seen but not addressed' problem where no one acts because responsibility is diffuse
Reply-all discipline: reply-all is appropriate when the response genuinely benefits everyone; otherwise it is spam and a sign of poor judgment
Signature and context: every external email should be answerable without the reader needing to look up who you are; include role, company, and a single contact method

Memo format for internal persistent communication: memos exist when an email thread is not durable enough; use them for policy changes, announcements, analytical findings, and anything that will be referenced later

Channel selection matrix: real-time needed plus low complexity equals chat; real-time needed plus high complexity equals call or video; async plus low complexity equals email; async plus high complexity plus permanence equals memo or document
The 'drafts folder overnight' rule for emotionally charged email: if you are angry, save as draft, sleep on it, re-read in the morning; you will almost always revise or not send it
lecture_notes.demonstration: Weak email: 'Subject: following up. Hi Sarah, hope you are doing well! I wanted to circle back on the conversation we had last week about the thing we discussed. I think there are some important points we should consider, and I was hoping we could touch base about next steps when you have a chance. Let me know your thoughts! Thanks, Mike.' Diagnosis: vague subject, no content, no request, no deadline. Rewrite: 'Subject: Decision needed by Thursday 5pm: approve $18K Salesforce contract? Sarah, I need your approval on the Salesforce contract we discussed last Tuesday so I can sign by Friday and lock in Q1 pricing. Cost: $18,000 annual, within the budget you approved in October. Risk: 30-day opt-out clause. Upside: retires three legacy tools, saving 12 hours per week across sales. Can you reply approved or lets-discuss by Thursday 5pm? If I do not hear back, I will book 15 minutes on your calendar Friday morning. Mike, Director of Sales Operations.' Discuss: the rewrite is longer but faster to read because it surfaces the decision, the structure, the deadline, and the fallback.
setup_requirements: Three anonymized weak emails from the instructor's archive for diagnostic rewriting
Subject line rubric: clear purpose, urgency, action, under 60 characters
ARD pattern cheat card for each student
Channel selection matrix handout

review.key: The Subject Line is the Email
review.value: Half your readers will decide whether to open based on the subject line alone. 'Quick question' and 'Update' are not subject lines; they are excuses. Promise the reader something specific and deliver it.
The first sentence is the most valuable real estate in any email. Do not waste it on 'Hope you are doing well.' Lead with the request, the decision, or the key fact.

If you have three unrelated requests, send three emails with three clear subject lines. Bundling them guarantees at least two will be forgotten and creates a thread that cannot be searched or forwarded cleanly.
If an email thread has gone more than three rounds without resolution, the email format is the problem. A two-minute phone call will resolve what ten emails cannot.

class_schedule.topic: Formal Business Reports: Informational and Analytical
learning_objectives: Distinguish informational reports (what happened) from analytical reports (what it means and what to do) and choose the right structure for each
Produce a report that opens with findings and recommendations, not with methodology and background
Apply the Pyramid Principle (Minto) to organize supporting evidence into mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive categories
Use headings, figures, and executive summaries so a busy reader can extract value at three levels of depth
readings: Locker & Kaczmarek, Business Communication, Ch. 18-19 - Reports and Proposals
Barbara Minto, The Pyramid Principle (3rd ed.), Ch. 1-4
Guffey & Loewy, Essentials of Business Communication, Ch. 11 - Reports, Proposals, and Presentations
HBR Guide to Better Business Writing, Ch. 10 - Structuring Long Documents

lecture_notes.introduction: In 1987, the Rogers Commission report on the Space Shuttle Challenger disaster included a famous appendix by physicist Richard Feynman. While the main report described the O-ring failure in careful technical detail spread across hundreds of pages, Feynman's appendix opened with a single devastating sentence: 'It appears that there are enormous differences of opinion as to the probability of a failure with loss of vehicle and of human life.' Management believed the probability of failure was 1 in 100,000. Working engineers believed it was closer to 1 in 100. Feynman led the reader from this staggering disagreement through the evidence, concluding 'For a successful technology, reality must take precedence over public relations, for nature cannot be fooled.' That appendix is studied in business schools because Feynman understood the governing principle of report writing: lead with the finding that matters, then walk the reader through the evidence in a structure they can follow. Most business reports do the opposite. They bury the lede under pages of background, methodology, and cover-your-ass qualifications, and they are consequently never read past page two. Today we learn to write reports that respect the executive's ten-minute ceiling: open with what we found, tell them what to do about it, and let the rest of the document support the case for those who want to dig deeper.
key_concepts: Informational vs analytical reports: informational reports describe a situation (monthly sales, project status, incident report) without recommending action; analytical reports diagnose a problem and recommend a decision (build vs buy, vendor selection, market entry)

The three-layer report: layer 1 is the executive summary (half a page, read in 30 seconds); layer 2 is the body with headings (skimmable in 3 minutes); layer 3 is appendices and supporting data (read in full only by the minority who need the detail)

Executive summary structure: the situation, the key finding or recommendation, the top three to five supporting points, and the requested decision; must stand alone so a reader who sees only the summary understands the full story
Pyramid Principle structure: a single governing idea at the top, supported by three to seven mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive (MECE) supporting ideas, each supported by evidence

MECE test: supporting points must not overlap (mutually exclusive) and must cover the full logical space (collectively exhaustive); overlapping points create double-counting; gaps create holes a skeptical reader will exploit

The SCQA opening (Situation, Complication, Question, Answer): situate the reader in a shared context, introduce the problem that makes the report necessary, pose the question the report answers, and answer it immediately
Headings as table of contents: headings should make sense as a standalone outline; 'Introduction' and 'Background' are filler; 'Why Our Win Rate Dropped 12% in Q3' teaches the reader the argument
Figures and tables earn their place: every figure must be referenced in the text, numbered, titled, and interpreted; decorative figures waste space and dilute attention

Recommendations are specific and actionable: 'improve customer experience' is not a recommendation; 'reduce average support response time from 4 hours to 30 minutes by adding two weekend-shift agents at $45K/year each' is
Risk and limitations section: every analytical report must honestly state what could go wrong and what the analysis cannot support; hiding risks makes readers trust you less, not more
lecture_notes.demonstration: Show two versions of an analytical report opening. Weak: 'Introduction. In recent quarters, our company has been experiencing various challenges in the customer retention space. This report was commissioned by the VP of Customer Success to investigate potential causes and explore possible avenues for improvement. The methodology involved reviewing internal data sources, conducting interviews with team members, and benchmarking against industry peers...' Diagnosis: no finding, no recommendation, no numbers, eighty words wasted before the reader learns anything. Strong: 'Recommendation: Cut the customer success team case load from 80 to 50 accounts per rep by end of Q2. This will reduce churn from 14% to a projected 9% and return roughly $4.2M in annual recurring revenue. The situation: Our churn rate climbed from 9% in Q1 2024 to 14% in Q3 2024, costing approximately $8.4M in ARR. The finding: 71% of churned customers last touched their CSM more than 45 days before cancellation, versus 12% of retained customers. Reps are carrying 80 accounts each, twice the industry norm of 40-50 (Gainsight benchmarks 2024).' The strong version states the recommendation, financial impact, problem, and root cause in the first 150 words.
setup_requirements: Sample analytical report with weak structure for in-class diagnosis
Pyramid Principle worksheet for organizing supporting points into MECE categories
Executive summary rubric (key finding, three supporting points, specific recommendation, financial impact)
Access to real published analytical reports from McKinsey, BCG, or Deloitte for structural reference

review.value: The executive reads the first paragraph. If you bury your recommendation on page 4, it does not exist. The strongest business reports answer the question in the first 150 words.

Supporting points that overlap cause double-counting. Points with gaps invite the skeptic to ask 'what about X?' and watch your argument crumble. Mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive is a discipline, not a nicety.
A reader who only reads your headings should understand your argument. 'Background' teaches nothing; 'Why the Old Process Fails at Scale' teaches the point.
Hiding limitations destroys your credibility the moment a sharp reader spots them. State the top two or three risks up front, and you will be trusted when the rest holds together.

learning_objectives: Structure a business proposal that answers the buyer's three unspoken questions: why act, why you, why now
Write an executive summary that sells the rest of the proposal in under 250 words
Quantify value using clear before-and-after metrics, not generic promises of 'improved efficiency'
Handle objections proactively in the proposal itself, rather than leaving them for the reader to raise
readings: Locker & Kaczmarek, Business Communication, Ch. 19 - Proposals
Guffey & Loewy, Essentials of Business Communication, Ch. 12 - Business Proposals
Minto, The Pyramid Principle, Ch. 5-6 on structuring persuasive documents
Tom Sant, Persuasive Business Proposals (3rd ed.), Ch. 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Every proposal, whether a consulting engagement letter, a software sales deck, an internal capital request, or a nonprofit grant application, is a persuasive document with exactly one job: get the reader to say yes and then act. Most proposals fail not because the underlying offer is weak, but because the document buries the offer under a fog of generic language, inward-focused company boilerplate, and value claims the reader has seen a hundred times. The buyer, whether a CFO reviewing a $2M software purchase or a foundation reviewing a $50K grant application, is reading ten to fifty similar documents and making gut-level decisions about which few to read carefully. The executive summary is the audition: if it does not make the reader want to read the body, the body never gets read. Today we learn the architecture of proposals that win: opening with the buyer's problem in the buyer's words, quantifying the cost of inaction, presenting a specific solution with specific outcomes, and handling the two or three objections the buyer is already thinking about before they become reasons to say no. We study real examples, including a side-by-side comparison of a weak software proposal that lost a deal and the rewritten version that the same team used to win a similar deal the following quarter.
key_concepts: The proposal's three questions: why should we do anything about this (cost of inaction), why should we choose you (differentiation and credibility), why should we act now (urgency without manufactured pressure)

Buyer-centered opening: start with the buyer's situation and problem in their own words, not with your company's founding year or mission statement; the buyer does not care about you until they believe you understand them

Quantified value: 'reduce customer support response time from 4 hours to 30 minutes, freeing 12 rep-hours per week, worth approximately $156,000 per year at a fully-loaded rate of $125 per hour'; generic claims like 'drive efficiency' are dismissed because every competitor makes them

The executive summary as standalone sales document: under 250 words, mirrors the structure of the full proposal (problem, solution, value, investment, next step), and must work if it is the only thing the decision maker reads
Problem statement discipline: describe the problem in terms the buyer has used (from discovery calls, RFP documents, or public statements), not in terms you would use; this signals that you listened
Solution specificity: name deliverables, timeline, people, tools, and measurable success criteria; vague solutions produce vague commitments and vague payment
Pricing presentation: show investment as tied to value ('$85K investment against $420K in year-one savings gives a 5x return and 3-month payback'); never present a raw price with no value anchor

Proactive objection handling: a section addressing the top three concerns the buyer is likely to raise (cost, risk, implementation complexity, competitor comparison) before they raise them; silence on these signals you have not thought about them

Social proof: one or two case studies from similar companies with specific quantified outcomes; vague testimonials ('they were great to work with') are worthless compared to 'we reduced onboarding time by 60% in 90 days'
The call to action: one specific next step with a name and a date, not 'please let me know your thoughts'; 'can we schedule a 30-minute call Thursday or Friday to confirm the scope?' gets a response
lecture_notes.demonstration: Weak executive summary: 'Acme Consulting is pleased to present this proposal for your consideration. Founded in 2003, we are a leading provider of digital transformation services with offices in five countries and a proven track record of delivering value to clients across multiple verticals. Our proprietary methodology combines industry best practices with cutting-edge technology to drive meaningful outcomes...' Diagnosis: 75 words in, the reader has learned nothing about their own problem or what they will get. Strong rewrite: 'Your Q2 earnings call named call-center wait times as the single biggest drag on NPS, which has fallen from 62 to 48 year-over-year. Our analysis of your publicly available data and a two-hour conversation with your head of customer care suggests roughly 40% of calls are routine status checks that could be resolved in self-service. We propose a 12-week engagement to design, build, and launch a self-service portal covering the top 20 call drivers. Based on comparable work at Delta Air Lines (42% call deflection in 16 weeks) and USAA (51% in 20 weeks), we expect to deflect 30-40% of call volume, saving roughly $3.2M annually against a $420K investment. First measurable impact in week 8. Next step: a 45-minute working session with your customer care and product teams on October 24 or 25 to confirm scope.' The rewrite sells in 190 words by citing the buyer's own words and naming specific comparables.
setup_requirements: Two real anonymized proposals (one that lost, one that won) for side-by-side analysis
Executive summary template (problem, solution, value, investment, call to action)
Value quantification worksheet for translating features into dollar impact
Objection-handling checklist for the top five concerns buyers raise

review.value: The buyer does not care about your company's mission or founding year. They care about their problem. Open with their situation in their words, and you immediately differentiate from the 90% of proposals that open with a self-portrait.
Generic value claims are invisible because every competitor makes them. Specific quantified outcomes are memorable and defensible. Numbers anchor decisions; adjectives do not.
The buyer is already thinking about cost, risk, timeline, and competitor comparison. Addressing these proactively builds trust; waiting for them to surface in a meeting makes you look defensive.
'Please let me know your thoughts' is a prayer, not a next step. 'Can we meet Thursday 2pm or Friday 10am to confirm scope?' gets a reply within 24 hours in 80% of cases.

class_schedule.topic: Persuasive Messages: AIDA and Influence Principles (Cialdini)
learning_objectives: Apply the AIDA framework (Attention, Interest, Desire, Action) to structure persuasive business messages
Apply Cialdini's six principles of influence (reciprocity, commitment, social proof, authority, liking, scarcity) ethically and diagnose their misuse
Distinguish indirect persuasive strategy (build the case, then ask) from direct strategy (ask first, then justify) and choose based on audience resistance
Recognize and avoid manipulative tactics that damage long-term trust for short-term compliance
readings: Robert Cialdini, Influence: The Psychology of Persuasion (new and expanded ed., 2021), Ch. 1-7
Guffey & Loewy, Essentials of Business Communication, Ch. 10 - Persuasive and Sales Messages
Locker & Kaczmarek, Business Communication, Ch. 11 - Persuasive Messages
Heath & Heath, Made to Stick (2007), Ch. 1-3 - SUCCESs framework

lecture_notes.introduction: In 1984, psychologist Robert Cialdini published Influence, a book based on three years of undercover fieldwork inside car dealerships, fundraising organizations, and telemarketing rooms. He documented that nearly all successful persuasion reduces to six repeatable principles, each rooted in deeply wired human heuristics. Forty years later, every good salesperson, every charity fundraiser, every political campaign, and every effective leader uses these principles, knowingly or not. The uncomfortable truth Cialdini emphasizes in the 2021 expanded edition is that the same principles that help a nonprofit raise money for real work can be weaponized by scammers and cult recruiters. That is why persuasion is not just a technical skill but an ethical one. Today we learn to structure persuasive business messages using AIDA, to deploy Cialdini's principles honestly, and to recognize when we or others are crossing the line from influence into manipulation. The distinction is clear and operational: ethical persuasion tells the truth and appeals to the audience's real interests; manipulation hides information or exploits cognitive shortcuts to get compliance against the audience's real interests. We study Steve Jobs' 2007 iPhone launch as a masterclass in ethical AIDA, and we contrast it with the Theranos investor pitch as a cautionary tale in manipulative authority and social proof.
key_concepts: AIDA structure: Attention (a hook that earns the reader's focus), Interest (a problem or stake the reader cares about), Desire (a vision of the solution that makes them want it), Action (a specific, easy next step)
Reciprocity: humans feel obligated to return a favor; a genuine gift or concession (a free resource, a helpful intro, a first draft of work) creates a bond that increases the chance of reciprocal yes

Commitment and consistency: once people take a small public step in a direction, they want to act consistently with it; 'would you be open to a 15-minute conversation?' is easier than 'sign this contract', and the small yes makes the big yes more likely

Social proof: people look to peers for guidance under uncertainty; 'three of your direct competitors already use this approach' is more persuasive than 'our product is great', because it anchors to peer behavior
Authority: people defer to credible experts; citing real research, real credentials, or real case studies (not fake ones) builds authority that vague claims cannot
Liking: people say yes more readily to people they like; genuine warmth, shared interests, and sincere compliments create the liking bond, but insincere flattery is detected and backfires

Scarcity: limited time or limited availability increases perceived value; 'this quote is valid until December 15' is honest scarcity, 'act now before it is too late' with no real deadline is manipulative scarcity

Unity (Cialdini's added seventh principle, 2016): people say yes to those they perceive as part of their in-group; invoking shared identity ('as fellow first-time founders') is powerful when genuine and alienating when fabricated

Direct vs indirect persuasive strategy: direct (ask first, justify second) for readers predisposed to say yes or who prefer brevity; indirect (build the case, then ask) for readers likely to object or who need persuading

The ethical line: influence becomes manipulation when you hide information the audience would use to make a different decision, or when you exploit principles against the audience's real interests; short-term wins through manipulation destroy long-term trust
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze Steve Jobs' iPhone keynote (January 9, 2007) using AIDA. Attention: 'Today Apple is going to reinvent the phone.' Interest: pain point of smartphones being smart but not easy to use, walked through with specific competitor product names. Desire: 'three revolutionary products: a widescreen iPod, a revolutionary mobile phone, and a breakthrough internet communicator' revealed as one device. Action: 'available in June... here is where to get in line.' Contrast with a typical weak product announcement: 'We are pleased to announce Acme 4.0, which includes new features and improvements to help you be more productive.' Diagnosis: no attention (no hook), no interest (no pain named), no desire (no specific benefit), no action (no dates or next step). Rewrite using AIDA with specific numbers and concrete benefits.
setup_requirements: Video access to Steve Jobs' 2007 iPhone keynote
Cialdini principles quick-reference card for each student
Sample persuasive messages (good and bad) for in-class diagnosis
AIDA structure worksheet

review.value: From a sales email to a TED talk to a wedding toast, the Attention-Interest-Desire-Action structure works because it matches how human attention and decision-making actually proceed. You can skip a stage, but you will lose your audience at that point.

'We are the best in our category' is discounted as self-promotion. '127 Fortune 500 CFOs use our platform, including 8 of the top 10 banks' is social proof that cannot be shrugged off. Name names when you can.

Manufactured urgency ('act now, limited time offer') trains sophisticated buyers to ignore you. Honest scarcity (a real deadline, a real constraint) respects the reader and accelerates decisions without damaging trust.

Before using any persuasion technique, ask: would I be comfortable if the audience saw exactly how I am trying to persuade them? If yes, proceed. If no, you are crossing into manipulation and your long-term reputation will pay the price.

class_schedule.topic: Bad News Messages: Direct vs Indirect Approaches
learning_objectives: Choose between the direct approach (bad news first) and the indirect approach (context first, then news) based on audience, relationship, and severity
Write bad news messages that preserve the recipient's dignity while being honest about the facts
Avoid the five classic bad-news failures: euphemism, blame-shifting, passive voice evasion, false sympathy, and buried leads
Apply the Domino's Pizza Turnaround framework of owning the problem publicly as a path to recovery
readings: Guffey & Loewy, Essentials of Business Communication, Ch. 9 - Negative Messages
Locker & Kaczmarek, Business Communication, Ch. 10 - Negative Messages
HBR Guide to Better Business Writing, Ch. 12 - Delivering Difficult News
Stone, Patton & Heen, Difficult Conversations (10th anniv. ed., 2010), Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: In December 2021, Better.com CEO Vishal Garg fired 900 employees, nine percent of his workforce, on a three-minute Zoom call just before Christmas. He began: 'If you are on this call, you are part of the unlucky group that is being laid off.' The video leaked within hours, Better.com's reputation collapsed, its planned IPO was delayed, and Garg took a temporary leave of absence. The layoffs themselves were defensible on business grounds; the delivery was not. Contrast this with Dara Khosrowshahi's 2020 Uber layoff announcement of 3,700 employees. He opened with the hard number, named the human cost explicitly, committed to severance and healthcare continuity, and took personal responsibility without euphemism. Both companies laid off people. One became a case study in how not to do it; the other preserved trust with remaining employees and the public. Today we learn to deliver bad news without destroying trust. The central insight is counterintuitive: the more honest and direct you are about the bad news itself, the more the audience will trust you on everything else. Softening language, hiding the lede, blame-shifting, and false sympathy all backfire because audiences detect them instantly and read them as either cowardice or contempt. We study the Domino's Pizza 'Our Pizza Sucks' campaign of 2009-2010 as the textbook example of how owning bad news about your own product can catalyze recovery, and we compare it with United Airlines' three disastrous statements about Dr. David Dao.
key_concepts: Direct approach: state the bad news in the first sentence or paragraph; appropriate when the reader needs the information immediately, when the relationship can bear directness, or when indirect framing would feel evasive

Indirect approach: build shared context first, explain the reasoning, then deliver the bad news, then end with what comes next; appropriate for emotionally severe news, unfamiliar audiences, or cultures where directness is read as rude

The buffer (indirect opening): a neutral, truthful statement that relates to the subject without false positivity; 'we have completed the Q4 review you requested' is a buffer; 'Thank you for your amazing submission!' before rejecting is false and insulting
Reasoning before news: in the indirect approach, explain the criteria or constraints first, then reveal that they were not met; this gives the recipient a fair sense of the decision process

Euphemism kills trust: 'workforce optimization', 'career change opportunity', 'right-sizing', 'synergy realization' all signal that the writer is afraid to name what is happening; audiences read these as cowardice and lose respect
Passive voice evasion: 'mistakes were made' and 'decisions were taken' hide the agent; naming who decided and why is harder but more trustworthy

Owning the problem: the Domino's approach (public admission that their pizza tasted bad, followed by visible product reformulation) produced a 14% same-store sales increase and a 234% stock gain over three years; denial would have produced neither

The dignity principle: even when delivering the hardest news, preserve the recipient's dignity; do not pile on, do not gloat, do not rub the outcome in, and do not make the recipient perform gratitude

Closing with agency: end with what the recipient can do next (severance details, next steps in the appeals process, a concrete resource); leaving them with nothing but the bad news maximizes despair without serving any purpose

Cultural adjustment: high-context cultures (Japan, Korea, much of the Middle East) often prefer indirect delivery; low-context cultures (Netherlands, Germany, US Northeast) often prefer directness; Meyer's Culture Map provides operational guidance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Weak bad-news letter (real anonymized example): 'Dear John, Thank you so much for your continued interest and patience throughout our extensive interview process. We were truly impressed by your background and enjoyed getting to know you. After much deliberation, we have decided to move forward with other candidates whose experience more closely aligns with our current needs. We wish you the very best in your job search and hope you will consider us for future opportunities.' Diagnosis: starts with false gratitude, buries the rejection in the middle, uses euphemism ('move forward with other candidates'), offers no actionable feedback, ends with an insulting invitation to try again. Thoughtful rewrite: 'Dear John, I am writing to let you know we are not moving forward with your application for the Senior Analyst role. You made it to our final round of three candidates, and the decision was genuinely close. The role went to a candidate with five years of direct experience in pharmaceutical benchmarking, which was a specific requirement for this position. Two things I can offer: first, I would be glad to give you a 15-minute call with candid feedback on your interviews (my calendar link below). Second, we have a Junior Analyst role opening in March that may be a better fit; I would be happy to flag your application if you are interested. I know this is disappointing news, and I appreciate the time you invested with us. Sincerely, Anna Chen, VP of Research.'
setup_requirements: Sample bad-news letters (weak and strong) for comparative analysis
Video clip of Dara Khosrowshahi's 2020 Uber layoff announcement
Video clip of the Better.com Zoom layoff for comparison
Domino's Pizza Turnaround case study and before/after ad campaigns

review.value: Audiences detect euphemism and false sympathy instantly. The more directly you name what is happening, the more trust you build. 'We are eliminating 42 positions' is more respectful than 'we are undertaking a strategic rightsizing initiative.'

Domino's admitted their pizza was bad in a national ad campaign and grew. Denial is the default response to bad news and the most expensive one. The path through bad news is almost always more honesty, not less.

Even when the recipient is in the wrong, even when you are defending a termination, even when you are rejecting a long-held dream, preserve their dignity. Cruelty in bad-news delivery creates enemies who will remember for years.

Always close with a concrete next step the recipient can take. Severance details, appeals process, alternate opportunity, or timeline for reconsideration. Bad news without a path forward is abandonment.

class_schedule.topic: Presentation Design: Structure and Visual Principles (Presentation Zen)
learning_objectives: Apply the Presentation Zen principles of simplicity, clarity, and focus to design slides that amplify rather than compete with the speaker
Design slide decks that follow a clear narrative arc (setup, conflict, resolution) rather than a bullet-point dump
Apply visual hierarchy, negative space, and the 1-1-1 rule (one idea, one image, one sentence per slide) to high-stakes slides
Diagnose and rewrite bullet-heavy slides into image-led, emotionally resonant alternatives
readings: Garr Reynolds, Presentation Zen (3rd ed., 2019), Ch. 1-5
Nancy Duarte, Slide:ology (2008), Ch. 1-4
Nancy Duarte, Resonate (2010), Ch. 1-3 - the presentation form
HBR Guide to Persuasive Presentations, Ch. 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: In 2001, Amazon CEO Jeff Bezos banned PowerPoint from executive meetings. In its place, he required six-page narrative memos read in silence at the start of each meeting. His reasoning, documented in shareholder letters and interviews, was that PowerPoint slides let presenters hide behind bullet points and graphics, avoiding the rigorous thinking that narrative prose demands. You cannot bluff your way through a six-page memo the way you can bluff your way through twenty slides. Bezos was half right. PowerPoint is indeed a terrible medium when it replaces thinking, which is what happens in most corporate decks: dense bullets, decorative clip art, template-driven layouts, and no actual argument. But PowerPoint is a powerful medium when it visually supports a well-constructed verbal narrative, which is what Steve Jobs demonstrated in every Apple keynote, what Al Gore used in An Inconvenient Truth, and what Hans Rosling used in his Gapminder TED talks. The difference is not the tool; the difference is whether the slides are crutches or amplifiers. Today we learn the Presentation Zen principles that separate amplifier slides from crutch slides. The central idea is subtraction: every element that does not directly serve the story should be removed, because slides with less on them force the audience to listen to you, which is what you want.
key_concepts: Slides are not documents: a slide viewed for 20 seconds must communicate one idea; a document can be read carefully over minutes; trying to do both creates 'slideuments' that fail at both
The 1-1-1 rule: one idea per slide, one image or chart per slide, one sentence (or fragment) per slide; decks that follow this rule typically triple in length but quadruple in retention
Visual hierarchy: the most important element on a slide should be the largest, brightest, or highest-contrast; competing focal points split audience attention
Negative space: empty space is not wasted space; it directs the eye, signals importance, and creates a sense of calm and intentionality; dense slides feel frantic
Image-led design: a single full-bleed photograph with a short caption is typically more memorable than a chart or bullet list, especially for emotional points
Typography discipline: two fonts maximum, large sizes (30pt minimum for body, 40pt+ for headings), high contrast with background; small type signals 'I am not sure you should read this'

The narrative arc: Nancy Duarte's presentation form alternates between 'what is' (current state) and 'what could be' (desired future), with a call to action at the end; this creates tension that pulls the audience through the talk
Slide as visual aid, not teleprompter: slides support what you say; they do not contain what you say; presenters who read slides aloud are redundant with their own visuals
The 10-20-30 rule (Guy Kawasaki): 10 slides, 20 minutes, 30-point minimum font; a constraint that forces prioritization and prevents death-by-PowerPoint
Consistency as discipline: one template, one color palette, one style of chart, one heading style throughout the deck; inconsistency signals carelessness and undermines credibility
lecture_notes.demonstration: Show two versions of a slide conveying 'Our customer support response time has dropped from 4 hours to 30 minutes since May.' Weak version: a slide titled 'Customer Support Performance Improvement Initiative Results Q2-Q3 2024' with a six-row table of monthly averages, three bullets of methodology, a decorative clip-art headset, the company logo, the date, the slide number, the confidentiality notice, and a footer. Diagnosis: 12 competing elements, no focal point, the audience reads instead of listens, and the key number is buried in the table. Strong version: a single enormous number '30 min' in the center of the slide, in large type, with the subtitle 'down from 4 hours in May' underneath. Nothing else. Discuss: the weak version is what 90% of corporate slides look like; the strong version is what Steve Jobs would put on screen. The presenter speaks the context; the slide carries the number.
setup_requirements: Access to Steve Jobs iPhone keynote slides for structural analysis
Nancy Duarte Resonate book chapter on presentation form
Before/after slide deck examples for redesign exercises
PowerPoint or Keynote for live slide redesign during class

review.value: If your slide can stand alone without a presenter, you have written a document and projected it. Slides support spoken narrative; documents replace it. Pick one and build for that medium.

Every element on a slide competes for attention. The Presentation Zen discipline is to remove everything that does not directly serve the point until the slide feels almost empty. That empty feeling is the sound of your audience listening to you.
A single giant number, image, or phrase is more memorable than a table of twelve small facts. If you must show the table, show it after the headline takeaway, not instead of it.

Mixed fonts, clashing colors, inconsistent chart styles, and scattered heading sizes all signal carelessness. A disciplined template is not aesthetic preference; it is evidence that you took the audience's attention seriously.

class_schedule.topic: Presentation Delivery: Voice, Body Language, Storytelling
learning_objectives: Apply vocal techniques (pace, pitch variation, strategic pauses, volume) to keep audiences engaged during live presentations
Use purposeful body language (stance, eye contact, gesture, movement) to reinforce rather than distract from the verbal message
Structure presentations using story arcs (setup-conflict-resolution) that hold attention through emotional engagement
Handle Q&A, hostile questions, and unexpected interruptions with composure and substance
readings: Reynolds, Presentation Zen, Ch. 6-9 - Delivery and Connecting with Audiences
Chris Anderson, TED Talks: The Official TED Guide to Public Speaking (2016), Ch. 1-7
Heath & Heath, Made to Stick, Ch. 6 - Stories
HBR Guide to Persuasive Presentations, Ch. 10-14 - Delivery

lecture_notes.introduction: In 2009, Simon Sinek walked onto a stage in Puget Sound for a TEDx talk about why some organizations inspire loyalty while others struggle to keep customers. He drew three concentric circles on a whiteboard labeled What, How, and Why. He spoke for 18 minutes without slides, without notes, and with a calm conversational pace that pulled the audience through a simple but rigorous argument. That talk, 'Start With Why', has been viewed over 60 million times and made Sinek one of the most booked speakers in the world. He is not a charismatic performer by nature. He is a thinker who worked hard at the craft of delivery. The lesson is that speaking effectively to a room is a learnable discipline, not a talent you are born with or without. The same principles that make Sinek compelling, that made Barack Obama's 2004 DNC keynote a career-launching moment, and that made Brene Brown's vulnerability TED talk a 60-million-view phenomenon, are within reach of any professional willing to practice. Today we cover the physical craft of live delivery: how to use your voice without exhausting it, how to use your body without distracting with it, and how to tell stories that make abstract points concrete and memorable. We also practice handling the hardest moment in any presentation: the hostile question from someone who wants you to fail.
key_concepts: Pace: the average conversational rate is 140-160 words per minute; presentation pace should be slightly slower (120-140 wpm) with deliberate pauses that feel long to you but feel natural to the audience
Pitch variation: monotone voices signal boredom and uncertainty; professional speakers vary pitch within a three- to five-note range across sentences to keep the ear engaged
Strategic pauses: a two-second pause after an important point gives the audience time to absorb and feels confident; filling every silence with 'um' and 'so' signals anxiety
Volume modulation: dropping volume slightly for emphasis pulls the audience in; rising volume for excitement signals enthusiasm; shouting signals loss of control
Planted feet, open stance: shifting weight and pacing signals anxiety; rooted stance with shoulders square to the audience signals confidence; this is learned, not innate

Eye contact as connection: hold eye contact with one person for the duration of one complete thought (about 3-5 seconds), then move to another; sweeping the room without resting creates the 'lighthouse' effect that connects with no one
Purposeful gesture: gestures should illustrate what you are saying or emphasize transitions; repetitive hand motions and pocket-hands signal nervousness; rehearsed gestures look fake

The story arc: setup (ordinary world), complication (something goes wrong or changes), resolution (how the problem was solved and what was learned); any abstract business point becomes more memorable when wrapped in a concrete story
The specific beats the general: 'our customer waited 47 minutes for support' is more memorable than 'our response times were slow'; concrete names, places, times, and numbers make stories stick

Q&A discipline: listen fully before answering, restate hostile questions in neutral terms before responding, acknowledge what is legitimate in the critique, redirect to evidence, never get defensive; the best Q&A technique is to actually answer the question asked rather than the question you wish was asked
lecture_notes.demonstration: Play two 60-second clips. First: a CEO at a town hall reading verbatim from a scripted statement, monotone, eyes on paper, volume flat, no pauses, no gestures. Second: Simon Sinek's opening 60 seconds of 'Start With Why' - conversational pace, deliberate pauses, varied pitch, eye contact with the camera/audience, simple purposeful gestures that illustrate his three circles. Run a stopwatch during each and count filler words ('um', 'so', 'like'), pauses over one second, and pitch variations. Discuss the measurable differences. Then have volunteers deliver a 60-second version of a key point from their assignment, video-record it, and play back. Most students are shocked at how fast they speak, how flat their pitch is, and how many fillers they use. This self-discovery is the fastest path to improvement.
setup_requirements: Video recording equipment for student practice deliveries
Access to Simon Sinek 'Start With Why' and Barack Obama 2004 DNC keynote for analysis
Filler-word and pause counting worksheet
Sample hostile questions for Q&A practice

review.key: Slow Down, Then Slow Down More
review.value: Nervous speakers race. Confident speakers pause. A two-second pause that feels endless to you is barely noticed by the audience, who will use it to absorb your last point. Pace is the single fastest upgrade to amateur delivery.

review.key: Eye Contact is One Person at a Time
review.value: Sweeping the room connects with no one. Hold one person's eyes for a complete thought, then move to another. A room of 500 people becomes a series of one-on-one conversations, and everyone feels seen.

Facts fade; stories stick. If you want your audience to remember one thing from your talk, wrap it in a concrete story with a specific person, a specific moment, and a specific turning point. Heath & Heath call this 'the Velcro theory of memory.'

In Q&A, the temptation is to pivot to what you wanted to say. The discipline is to answer what was actually asked. Politicians who dodge lose trust; professionals who engage directly build it, even when they disagree with the questioner.

class_schedule.topic: Visual Communication: Charts, Dashboards, and Chart Junk (Tufte)
learning_objectives: Apply Edward Tufte's principles (data-ink ratio, chart junk elimination, small multiples) to business charts and dashboards
Choose the right chart type for the question being asked (comparison, composition, distribution, relationship, change over time)
Avoid the six most common chart crimes: truncated axes, 3D effects, dual-axis overlays, pie charts for many slices, rainbow palettes, and decorative backgrounds
Design executive dashboards that surface the two or three metrics that matter, not the twenty that could be tracked
readings: Edward Tufte, The Visual Display of Quantitative Information (2nd ed.), Ch. 1-5
Stephen Few, Show Me the Numbers (2nd ed.), Ch. 1-6
Cole Nussbaumer Knaflic, Storytelling with Data (2015), Ch. 1-5
Alberto Cairo, The Truthful Art (2016), Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: On January 28, 1986, NASA management decided to launch the Space Shuttle Challenger in near-freezing temperatures despite warnings from Thiokol engineers that the O-ring seals could fail in the cold. The Challenger exploded 73 seconds into flight, killing all seven crew members. In the subsequent investigation, Edward Tufte analyzed the chart the engineers had used to try to convince management not to launch. The chart was a confusing multi-panel display with 13 O-ring erosion incidents scattered across no particular axis, no temperature axis at all, and no line showing the trend. The engineers had the data that would have made the case (strong correlation between cold temperature and O-ring damage), but they failed to present it in a chart that revealed the pattern. Tufte's redrawn version, a simple scatter plot of erosion damage vs temperature, shows the pattern so clearly that any reasonable reader would have aborted the launch. Tufte's conclusion, devastating and correct, is that poor charts do not just fail to inform; they can kill people. Today we learn the principles of rigorous chart design: how to choose the right chart type for the question, how to remove visual elements that do not carry information, and how to avoid the common crimes that make business charts misleading. This is not aesthetic preference; it is professional responsibility.
key_concepts: Data-ink ratio (Tufte): the ratio of ink used to show data to ink used for decoration; maximize data ink, minimize non-data ink; remove gridlines, backgrounds, borders, and 3D effects unless they carry information
Chart junk: any visual element that does not convey data; includes decorative clip art, gradient backgrounds, drop shadows, 3D perspective, rainbow color palettes, and redundant labels

Chart type by question: comparison across categories equals bar chart; change over time equals line chart; parts of a whole equals stacked bar or treemap (rarely pie); distribution equals histogram or box plot; relationship between two variables equals scatter plot
The pie chart problem: humans cannot compare angles accurately; pie charts with more than three or four slices become unreadable; a sorted horizontal bar chart is almost always clearer

Truncated axis fraud: starting a bar chart's y-axis at anything other than zero exaggerates differences; used constantly in misleading business presentations; always start bar charts at zero, and note when you deviate
Dual-axis overlays: overlaying two different metrics on two y-axes invites false correlation; avoid unless the two metrics are on the same scale (e.g., two revenue streams)
Color with purpose: use color to highlight the most important data point, not to decorate; gray for baseline data, bright color for the point you want the reader to see; rainbow palettes are noise

Small multiples (Tufte): a grid of many small charts, each showing the same variable for a different category; lets the reader compare dozens of series at once without cognitive overload; used by The New York Times, The Economist, and the best data journalism
Direct labeling: label data points directly rather than using a separate legend; the reader's eye should not have to jump between chart and legend to decode colors

The dashboard discipline: an executive dashboard should show the two or three metrics that directly reflect the business's health, not the twenty metrics that are easy to pull; more metrics equals less signal
lecture_notes.demonstration: Show a real corporate dashboard: 18 charts crammed onto one screen, each with 3D effects, rainbow palettes, truncated axes, unreadable legends, pie charts with 12 slices, and dual-axis overlays. Walk through identifying every Tufte violation. Then rebuild the dashboard with Tufte principles: three charts (not 18), bar charts instead of pies, zero-based axes, gray baselines with one highlighted metric, direct labels instead of legends, and clean typography. The redesigned dashboard tells a clearer story in a fraction of the visual noise. Discuss how this is the default output when a team is told 'show everything' instead of 'show what matters', and how asking 'what is the one decision this dashboard should inform?' reshapes the design.
setup_requirements: Sample corporate dashboards for in-class redesign
Tufte's Challenger chart analysis for case study
Tableau, Excel, or Google Sheets for live chart reconstruction
Color accessibility checker (for colorblind-safe palettes)

review.value: Every pixel in your chart should either show data or make data easier to read. Everything else is noise. The Tufte discipline is to subtract until only the signal remains.
Humans cannot compare angles; we can compare lengths. A sorted horizontal bar chart beats a pie chart for nearly every question except 'is this slice more or less than half of the whole?'

Truncated axes on bar charts are the single most common form of chart deception. A bar chart must start at zero; a line chart can start anywhere if it preserves the reader's ability to judge proportional change.

The dashboard reflex is to add more metrics. The professional discipline is to remove metrics until only the ones that drive decisions remain. A dashboard with three meaningful metrics beats a dashboard with thirty forgettable ones.

class_schedule.topic: Meeting Facilitation and Productive Group Communication
learning_objectives: Design and run meetings with clear purpose, agenda, and expected outcomes
Apply facilitation techniques to balance participation and keep discussion on track
Distinguish among decision-making methods (consensus, majority, consultative, authority) and match them to situations
Document meetings with action items that survive the meeting itself
readings: Guffey & Loewy, Essentials of Business Communication (12th ed.), Ch. 11 - Professionalism at Work
Schwarz, The Skilled Facilitator (new and revised ed., 2016), Ch. 1-3
Lencioni, Death by Meeting (2004), Part I

lecture_notes.introduction: Meetings are the largest single expenditure of managerial time and one of the most frequently cited sources of frustration in working life. A 2019 Harvard Business Review survey found that executives spend an average of 23 hours per week in meetings, up from less than 10 hours in the 1960s. Most of those meetings are judged unproductive by the participants. The problem is not meetings per se — collaborative work requires synchronous conversation some of the time — but the default culture of meetings in most organizations: no agenda, unclear purpose, wrong participants, no decisions, no follow-up. A well-run meeting is a rare and valuable thing. It requires deliberate preparation before, skilled facilitation during, and disciplined documentation after. Jeff Bezos famously banned slide decks at Amazon meetings in favor of six-page narrative memos, arguing that writing a memo forces clearer thinking than bullet points. The first 20 minutes of each meeting are spent silently reading the memo together. The rule is controversial but the underlying insight is sound: better preparation produces better meetings. This session focuses on the practical skills of running meetings that people actually want to attend, including how to decide whether a meeting is needed at all, how to design an agenda, how to facilitate different kinds of conversations (brainstorming, decision-making, status, retrospective), how to handle difficult participants, and how to make sure decisions stick after everyone leaves the room.
key_concepts: Meeting purpose taxonomy: status (share information), decision (choose among options), problem-solving (explore and resolve), brainstorming (generate ideas), planning (coordinate action), relationship (build trust); each type requires different preparation and facilitation

The first question: does this need to be a meeting? Many meetings should be emails, Slack threads, or async documents; Shopify's meeting cancellation initiative in 2023 deleted thousands of recurring meetings and saved millions of hours
Agenda structure: topic, owner, desired outcome, time allocation; distributed in advance; specific enough that participants can prepare

Decision-making methods: consensus (everyone agrees), majority (vote wins), consultative (leader decides after hearing input), authority (leader decides unilaterally); the wrong method for the situation produces waste or resentment

Facilitator vs participant: the facilitator's role is process, not content; they manage time, draw out quiet voices, redirect tangents, summarize agreement; hybrid facilitator/participant roles create conflicts of interest
RACI or similar role clarity: Responsible (does the work), Accountable (owns the outcome), Consulted (input sought), Informed (kept updated); confusion about roles is a major meeting dysfunction

Handling difficult participants: the dominator (use silent voting, round-robin), the silent observer (direct invitation), the derailer (park off-topic items in a parking lot), the pessimist (acknowledge concern, redirect to solution)

Amazon's 'silent start' meeting format: read a 6-page narrative memo together at the start, then discuss; forces better preparation and deeper thinking than slide decks; used in all senior meetings including S-team
Meeting documentation: written action items with owner, deliverable, and deadline; distributed within 24 hours; reviewed at start of next meeting; the single highest-leverage meeting practice

Remote and hybrid meeting challenges: presence inequality (remote participants get less airtime), technical issues, fatigue from video; solutions include all-remote or all-in-person rules, camera norms, structured turn-taking
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare two real meeting styles: (1) A typical 'status update' meeting where each person goes around the table describing what they are working on — often 45 minutes of information that could have been an email, with participants zoning out when others speak. (2) Amazon's 'silent start' S-team meeting: for the first 20 minutes, everyone reads a 6-page narrative memo in silence; then discussion begins with informed participants asking substantive questions. Analyze: which produces better decisions? Which is more respectful of time? What are the trade-offs (preparation cost, introvert vs extrovert dynamics, writing skill requirements)? Then work through a good vs bad agenda for a hypothetical product launch planning meeting, showing how the specific outcome statement ('decide launch date and communication plan') shapes the entire meeting differently from a vague one ('discuss launch').
setup_requirements: Meeting agenda template
RACI matrix template
Sample action-item tracker

review.value: The best meeting is the one you didn't need to have; before scheduling, ask whether the goal could be achieved with an email, a shared document, or an async Slack thread
A well-prepared agenda with pre-read materials eliminates most meeting problems; you cannot rescue an unprepared meeting with clever facilitation
Confusion about whether a meeting is making a decision by consensus or consultation is the most common source of post-meeting conflict; state it at the start
A meeting without documented action items is a meeting that did not happen; document owner, what, when; circulate within 24 hours

class_schedule.topic: Cross-Cultural Communication: Hofstede, Hall, and Meyer
learning_objectives: Apply Hall's high-context vs low-context framework to predict communication style differences
Use Hofstede's cultural dimensions to anticipate cross-cultural misunderstandings
Apply Erin Meyer's eight-scale culture map to navigate international business communication
Adapt communication approaches without stereotyping individuals
readings: Meyer, The Culture Map: Breaking Through the Invisible Boundaries of Global Business (2014), Ch. 1-4
Hall, Beyond Culture (1976), selected chapters on context
Hofstede, Hofstede & Minkov, Cultures and Organizations: Software of the Mind (3rd ed., 2010), Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Communication that works perfectly in one culture can fail or offend in another. An American manager giving feedback directly ('Your report has three major problems — fix them by Friday') may be seen as efficient and honest by another American but as brutally rude by a Japanese colleague who expected the feedback wrapped in layers of indirect language and context. A German engineer's exhaustive technical precision in an email may feel rigid and untrusting to a Brazilian counterpart, who expected relationship-building small talk before business. These are not matters of individual personality; they are patterns rooted in cultural norms about context, hierarchy, time, emotion, and trust. As business becomes more global and remote, cross-cultural communication becomes a routine workplace skill, not a specialized one. This session covers the three frameworks most commonly used to make cultural patterns visible: Edward Hall's high-context/low-context distinction (1976), Geert Hofstede's six cultural dimensions (derived from IBM data across 40+ countries), and Erin Meyer's Culture Map (2014) which extends both with eight practical scales for business communication. Used carefully, these frameworks help you anticipate where misunderstanding is likely and adapt your style accordingly. Used carelessly, they become stereotypes that blind you to individual variation within cultures — Meyer's advice is to learn the patterns, then expect exceptions.
key_concepts: Hall's high-context vs low-context: high-context cultures (Japan, China, Saudi Arabia, France) communicate through shared understanding, nonverbal cues, and implicit meaning; low-context cultures (US, Germany, Scandinavia) communicate through explicit verbal messages

Hofstede's six dimensions: Power Distance (acceptance of hierarchy), Individualism vs Collectivism, Masculinity vs Femininity (achievement vs cooperation), Uncertainty Avoidance (comfort with ambiguity), Long-term vs Short-term Orientation, Indulgence vs Restraint

Erin Meyer's Culture Map — 8 scales: Communicating (low-context vs high-context), Evaluating (direct vs indirect negative feedback), Persuading (principles-first vs applications-first), Leading (egalitarian vs hierarchical), Deciding (consensual vs top-down), Trusting (task-based vs relationship-based), Disagreeing (confrontational vs avoids confrontation), Scheduling (linear vs flexible time)

Negative feedback styles: Dutch and Israeli cultures give direct negative feedback in front of colleagues and see it as a sign of respect; Japanese and Indonesian cultures consider direct negative feedback humiliating and prefer private, softened delivery; Americans sit in between and often confuse European colleagues

Task-based vs relationship-based trust: US and Northern European cultures build trust primarily through work competence ('you delivered, I trust you'); Chinese, Russian, and Middle Eastern cultures require relationship investment first ('we had dinner together, drank, learned about each other's families')

Time orientation: linear-time cultures (Germany, Switzerland, Scandinavia) plan carefully, stick to schedules, value punctuality; flexible-time cultures (many African, Middle Eastern, Latin American) treat schedules as approximate and value responsiveness to current events

Power distance and decision styles: high power distance cultures expect decisions from the top and consider consensus processes inefficient; low power distance cultures expect participation and consider autocratic decisions illegitimate

Relative positioning, not absolute: Meyer stresses that what matters is where your culture sits RELATIVE to the other culture, not the absolute position; a Dutch-German interaction is very different from a Dutch-Indonesian one
Individual variation within cultures: cultural patterns describe averages; any individual may fall anywhere on a scale; use patterns to anticipate and adapt, not to predict specific people's behavior

Code-switching: skilled cross-cultural communicators adapt their style by context; the same person may give directly delivered feedback to a Dutch colleague and indirectly nuanced feedback to a Japanese colleague, without being inauthentic
lecture_notes.demonstration: Work through two real cross-cultural incidents from Meyer's book and discuss how to handle each: (1) A French engineer sends a detailed email critiquing an Indian team's code quality. The Indian team is deeply offended and relationships break down. Analyze using the Evaluating scale: France tends toward direct negative feedback, while India tends toward indirect. The French engineer's direct tone was normal for him and unacceptable in context. Remediation: the French manager should rewrite the feedback using indirect phrasing, deliver in person or via video call with relationship-building opening, and explicitly express respect for the team's effort. (2) A Brazilian project manager holds a consensual decision-making style, so she gathers input from her German team members and expects them to argue views openly before she synthesizes. The Germans wait politely for her to decide and are frustrated that she is 'wasting time'. Analyze using Deciding scale: Brazilian culture tends consensual, German tends top-down with expert input. Both are legitimate styles but they grind against each other. Remediation: explicit conversation about decision method at the start of the project.
setup_requirements: Erin Meyer Culture Map diagram and 8-scale summary
Hofstede country comparison data (hofstede-insights.com)
Cross-cultural case scenarios

review.value: The same message can be delivered in different cultural styles; what sounds rude in one culture may be normal in another, and vice versa; content and delivery are separable

What feels direct to a Japanese person may feel indirect to an American; what feels formal to an Australian may feel casual to a German; always think about where you are relative to the other party, not in absolute terms
Culture gives you a prior expectation, not a prediction; treat frameworks as hypotheses to test, not stereotypes to apply mechanically; always update based on the actual person
When working cross-culturally, spend time up front explicitly discussing communication and decision norms; making the implicit explicit prevents most misunderstandings

class_schedule.topic: Digital Communication: Email, Slack, Video, and Asynchronous Norms
learning_objectives: Match digital channels (email, Slack/Teams, video, async docs) to message types using channel richness theory
