Apply etiquette and best practices for asynchronous team communication
Structure video calls and remote meetings for high engagement
Design team communication norms that scale across time zones
readings: Guffey & Loewy, Essentials of Business Communication (12th ed.), Ch. 3 - Communication in the Digital Age
GitLab, 'GitLab Remote Playbook' (public handbook on async-first communication)
Stone & Brown, 'The Ideal Team Player in a Remote World,' HBR (September 2020)

lecture_notes.introduction: The digital transformation of business communication is the largest shift in how people work together since the telephone. In 2000, most business communication was face-to-face or by telephone, with email as a growing but secondary channel. By 2025, most professional communication is text-based, asynchronous, and mediated by software: email, Slack, Microsoft Teams, Notion, Google Docs, async video (Loom), ticketing systems, and countless others. Each channel has different norms, speeds, and costs, and using the wrong channel for a message is a common source of friction. A nuanced, emotional conversation delivered in a Slack thread will almost always go worse than the same conversation in video or in person. A simple status update delivered in a video call wastes everyone's time when a Slack message would do. The COVID-19 pandemic accelerated the shift to remote and hybrid work and made digital communication literacy a core competency rather than a specialty. Remote-first companies like GitLab, Automattic, and Basecamp have published extensive handbooks on how to make async-first communication work. The key insight is that distributed teams cannot rely on hallway conversations, so they must be intentional about writing things down, establishing communication norms, and choosing channels deliberately. This session covers the practical mechanics of digital channels, along with the emerging norms of remote and hybrid work communication.
key_concepts: Channel richness revisited: face-to-face is richest (multiple cues, immediate feedback, personal presence), video is second, phone third, synchronous chat fourth, email fifth, formal reports least rich; choose based on message ambiguity, emotion, and stakes
Email for: durable records, external communication, formal messages, multi-party distribution, messages that need to be thoughtful; avoid for: urgent items, emotional conversations, nuanced debate
Slack/Teams for: fast informal updates, quick questions, team banter, coordination; avoid for: decisions that need durability, sensitive or emotional topics, onboarding materials
Video calls for: emotional conversations, complex decisions, relationship building, high-ambiguity problems; avoid for: status updates, content anyone could read faster on their own

Async-first principle: default to asynchronous communication (written, not meeting) and only move synchronous when genuinely needed; supported by time-zone diversity, deep-work protection, and documentation
GitLab's async-first culture: 60+ countries, single 8,000-page handbook, all proposals written first, minimal scheduled meetings; shows async-first can work at scale
Written communication premium in remote work: because tone is lost in text, written communication must be more explicit, more careful with phrasing, and more generous with context than spoken
Time zone considerations: follow-the-sun workflows, respect of core hours, recording of meetings for those who cannot attend live, async-first to avoid disadvantaging people in off-hours time zones

Video call best practices: camera on as default (unless specified otherwise), mute when not speaking, name at bottom of frame, good lighting and audio, background control, start on time, end 5 minutes early to allow transition

Digital communication norms that teams can set: response time expectations by channel (Slack 4 hours, email 24 hours, urgent via phone), boundaries on after-hours messaging, escalation procedures, documentation of decisions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through GitLab's async-first 'single source of truth' philosophy: every process, every decision, every piece of institutional knowledge lives in a public handbook. When someone asks a question in Slack, the default response is 'Did you check the handbook? If the answer isn't there, let's update it together.' This eliminates repeated questions, makes new hire onboarding self-service, and provides a permanent institutional memory that survives individual departures. Then show a real example: a decision about moving to a new deployment pipeline is made not in a meeting but as a proposal document that is reviewed async, commented on by stakeholders, revised based on feedback, and then approved. The whole process is visible to everyone in the company, including people who were not directly involved. Discuss the trade-offs: it requires more writing skill, slows down fast decisions, and can feel impersonal. But it scales to thousands of employees across all time zones and eliminates the information asymmetry of who was in the meeting vs who wasn't.
setup_requirements: GitLab handbook sample pages
Channel selection decision tree
Remote work communication norms template

review.key: Channel Choice Is a Communication Skill
review.value: Knowing which channel to use for which message is as important as knowing what to say; wrong-channel choices cause most digital communication friction

review.key: Default Async, Escalate Sync When Needed
review.value: Async-first protects deep work, supports distributed teams, and produces better decisions; use sync (meetings, calls) only when async cannot do the job

review.key: Write for People Who Are Not in the Room
review.value: In remote work, everything important should be written down in a durable place; conversations in Slack DMs leave no institutional memory and exclude anyone outside the thread
Rather than enforcing rigid policies, establish shared norms on response time, channels, and boundaries; norms scale; rules do not

learning_objectives: Apply the crisis communication model (transparency, speed, empathy, action) to real cases
Use the Difficult Conversations framework (Stone, Patton, Heen) to navigate high-stakes interpersonal conversations
Distinguish effective apologies from non-apologies and corporate-speak
Design a crisis communication response for a realistic scenario
readings: Stone, Patton & Heen, Difficult Conversations: How to Discuss What Matters Most (10th anniv. ed., 2010), Ch. 1-5
Coombs, Ongoing Crisis Communication: Planning, Managing, and Responding (6th ed., 2023), Ch. 1-2 (Situational Crisis Communication Theory)
Lagadec, Preventing Chaos in a Crisis (1993), selected excerpts

lecture_notes.introduction: Crisis communication is the test of a company's character and its leadership's courage. When things go badly wrong — a product defect kills customers, a data breach exposes millions of users, an executive is caught in misconduct, a plant explodes — the first 24 hours shape the reputation of the company for years. The wrong response, even accompanied by sincere apology and eventual correction, can destroy trust that took decades to build. The right response can paradoxically strengthen the company's reputation by demonstrating competence and values under pressure. Two textbook cases anchor the discussion. Johnson & Johnson's 1982 response to the Tylenol cyanide poisonings — in which someone tampered with bottles on store shelves, killing seven people — is considered the gold standard. J&J immediately recalled 31 million bottles at a cost of $100 million, held daily press briefings, worked with the FDA and law enforcement, and redesigned packaging industry-wide. The company's stock recovered within a year and the Tylenol brand remains strong today. BP's 2010 response to the Deepwater Horizon oil spill is the opposite lesson. CEO Tony Hayward downplayed the magnitude ('very modest'), focused on the company's inconvenience ('I'd like my life back'), and appeared evasive in Congressional testimony. BP lost $60 billion in market value and Hayward was forced out. The same events could have been managed very differently. This session covers the principles of crisis communication and the related skill of difficult interpersonal conversations.
key_concepts: Crisis communication imperatives: transparency (don't hide or spin facts), speed (first 24 hours matter most), empathy (acknowledge impact on victims), action (concrete steps being taken), consistency (one spokesperson, one message)

Situational Crisis Communication Theory (Coombs): crisis type determines appropriate response; victim cluster (natural disaster, tampering) calls for sympathy; accidental cluster (accident, challenge) calls for diminishment; preventable cluster (self-caused) calls for rebuild (apology, compensation)

The bad-apology pattern: non-specific ('mistakes were made'), conditional ('if anyone was offended'), deflecting ('tough environment'), self-pitying ('this has been hard on us'); recognizable immediately and damaging always

Effective apology components: clearly name what happened, take responsibility without qualification, acknowledge harm to specific people, explain what happened and what you learned, commit to specific remediation, follow through

Tylenol 1982 as textbook: recalled all 31M bottles ($100M cost, criticized by investors as overreaction), daily media briefings, partnered with FDA and FBI, redesigned packaging with tamper-evident seals (industry-wide standard), CEO visibly leading

BP Deepwater Horizon 2010 as cautionary tale: minimized severity ('very modest'), appeared evasive, tone-deaf comments ('I'd like my life back'), slow response to media questions, CEO seen as out of touch; result: brand damage, stock collapse, CEO firing

Difficult conversations (Stone/Patton/Heen): three conversations happen at once — the 'what happened' conversation (facts and fault), the 'feelings' conversation (emotions), and the 'identity' conversation (what this says about who I am); failing to recognize all three leads to poor outcomes

Shift from blame to contribution: blame is about fault and punishment; contribution is about how each person's actions contributed to the situation, which opens space for problem-solving rather than defensiveness

The crisis communication war room: a dedicated group including PR, legal, HR, operations, and the CEO; meets continuously during a crisis; coordinates messages across all channels; prevents the inconsistency that makes bad crises worse
Social media amplification: modern crises move at the speed of Twitter; companies have hours, not days, to respond; a delayed or poorly-crafted initial response can dominate coverage permanently
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare two real crisis responses side by side: (1) J&J Tylenol 1982 — within 6 days of the first death, J&J had recalled all 31 million bottles nationwide, offered product exchanges at no cost, established a hotline, opened the investigation to media, and worked with regulators. CEO James Burke was visibly present in daily press briefings. The company absorbed $100M in direct costs and criticism from Wall Street for overreacting. One year later, Tylenol had recovered most of its market share and J&J's reputation for integrity was strengthened. (2) Better.com December 2021 — CEO Vishal Garg fired 900 employees (9% of workforce) via a 3-minute Zoom call: 'If you're on this call, you are part of the unlucky group being laid off. Your employment here is terminated effective immediately.' Video leaked to TikTok within hours. Garg called the group 'lazy' in a separate internal memo. The company's reputation collapsed, recruiting became nearly impossible, investors demanded governance changes, and the already-delayed IPO was further postponed. What distinguishes J&J from Better.com is not just the decisions but the communication: J&J led with empathy and action; Better.com led with contempt and logistics. Students should be able to articulate the specific communication choices that made one case admired and the other notorious.
setup_requirements: Crisis communication timeline templates
Tylenol and Better.com case backgrounds
Difficult Conversations framework handout

review.value: The initial response to a crisis shapes the entire narrative; delayed or evasive first responses cannot be undone by later good behavior
Trying to minimize or hide bad facts is always worse than acknowledging them; the facts come out eventually and the cover-up adds its own scandal

review.key: Apologize for Harm, Not for Feelings
review.value: 'I'm sorry you were offended' is a non-apology that blames the offended party; 'I'm sorry for what we did' takes responsibility and is the only apology that rebuilds trust
Every hard conversation has factual, emotional, and identity dimensions; addressing only one layer leaves the others festering; skilled communicators work on all three

class_schedule.topic: Personal Brand, Professional Presence, and Course Synthesis
learning_objectives: Articulate a personal communication brand grounded in authenticity and consistency
Evaluate and improve your LinkedIn profile, resume, and professional online presence
Synthesize the course into a personal communication improvement plan
Apply emerging trends (AI writing assistants, video ubiquity, remote defaults) to your practice
readings: Clark, Reinventing You: Define Your Brand, Imagine Your Future (updated ed., 2017), Ch. 1-4
Goldsmith, What Got You Here Won't Get You There (2007), Part I
Guffey & Loewy, Essentials of Business Communication (12th ed.), Ch. 16 (Employment Communication)

lecture_notes.introduction: Every communication you make contributes to your professional reputation. Over time, these individual messages accumulate into a 'personal brand' — what colleagues, clients, and potential employers think of when they think of you. Personal brand is not about self-promotion or slick LinkedIn posts; it is the accumulated impression of how you actually show up in writing and speech. Someone who consistently writes clear, respectful emails, runs meetings that people enjoy attending, delivers presentations people remember, and handles difficult conversations with maturity builds a reputation for strong communication that opens doors. Someone who writes sloppy messages, runs wandering meetings, and avoids difficult conversations builds the opposite reputation, regardless of their technical skills. This final session is about taking the tools from fourteen weeks of course material and integrating them into your own practice — not as a checklist to follow mechanically, but as a set of habits that become part of how you work. The session also looks forward. AI writing assistants (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini) are reshaping how professionals draft emails, reports, and presentations. Used well, they eliminate drudgery and help non-native English writers reach parity. Used badly, they produce generic, bloated content that no one wants to read and nobody trusts. Video communication is becoming as common as written. Remote and hybrid work make written clarity more important than ever. Your communication skills need to evolve continuously, and this course is a foundation rather than a destination.
key_concepts: Personal brand defined: the sum of impressions others form from your communications and behavior; built daily through small consistent actions rather than through one-time branding exercises
Authenticity as foundation: a personal brand must match who you actually are; performed or fake brands collapse under pressure and alienate people; clarity about your values is the starting point

LinkedIn as primary professional platform: 900+ million users, used by 95% of recruiters; your profile is often the first impression people have of you; optimize headline, summary, experience, and recommendations
Resume principles: one page for early career, two pages max for experienced; quantified achievements (numbers, percentages, scale); action verbs; avoid jargon; tailored to each role
Elevator pitch: 30-60 second self-introduction; answers 'what do you do and why should I care'; tailored to audience; not memorized verbatim but practiced
Online presence audit: Google yourself regularly; remove or address anything embarrassing or outdated; claim your name on major platforms even if you don't use them actively

AI writing assistants as tools, not replacements: useful for brainstorming, outlining, grammar checking, simplifying complex text; dangerous if used to generate content you don't review and own; developing a critical eye for AI-generated bloat is a new literacy
Video ubiquity: asynchronous video (Loom, Vidyard) is replacing some email and text; live video calls are a default not a special event; presentation skills are more important than ever
Continuous improvement model: every communication is practice; after each important email, meeting, or presentation, ask what went well and what could be better next time; small improvements compound

The communication learning plan: identify your two or three biggest weaknesses, find specific practices to address them, commit to a deliberate practice schedule, measure improvement; treat communication like any other professional skill that can be developed
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each student completes a personal communication assessment with three components: (1) STRENGTHS — identify two communication skills where you consistently receive positive feedback (e.g., clear writing, strong presentations, active listening); (2) WEAKNESSES — identify two where you struggle or get corrective feedback (e.g., giving difficult feedback, running meetings, writing executive summaries); (3) IMPROVEMENT PLAN — for each weakness, identify one specific practice you will do monthly for the next year (e.g., 'I will practice giving one piece of honest negative feedback per week in my team meetings,' 'I will prepare a one-page pre-read for every meeting I schedule'). Share in small groups for accountability. Then close the course by returning to the opening question from week 1: how is communication the foundation of everything else in business? Invite students to articulate, now with 14 weeks of evidence, why investing in communication skills has a higher career ROI than almost any other professional investment.
setup_requirements: Personal communication assessment worksheet
LinkedIn profile rubric
Resume and elevator pitch templates

review.value: Every message, every meeting, every presentation contributes; you do not build a brand in one big move, you build it in a thousand small ones
A genuine, competent, consistent communicator outperforms a polished but inauthentic one; do not try to be someone you are not

review.key: AI Is a Drafting Tool, Not a Thinking Tool
review.value: Use AI to draft, outline, and revise; never use it to replace your judgment or your ownership of the message; edit everything it produces
Technical skills may get you hired; communication skills determine how high you rise; invest in communication the way you invest in your core craft

questions.question: A marketing manager sends the following email to her team: 'Hey team, heads up we need to think about the Q2 campaign at some point this week hopefully, lets try to get aligned on direction I think we're behind. Let me know your thoughts whenever, thx!' (a) Identify at least five problems with this email. (b) Rewrite it as a clear, actionable, professional message. (c) Explain why the rewrite is more effective.
questions.answer: (a) Problems: (1) No specific deadline — 'this week' plus 'hopefully' plus 'whenever' gives no actionable timeline; (2) Vague subject/purpose — 'think about Q2 campaign' doesn't specify what decision or input is needed; (3) No clear ask — is she requesting a meeting, written input, or just awareness? (4) Undefined 'direction' — what direction needs alignment? brand? budget? channel mix? (5) Subjective language — 'I think we're behind' — behind what, by whose measure? (6) Informal tone overall ('Hey,' 'thx,' no capitalization) undermines the urgency she is trying to create; (7) No action items or owners; (8) Subject line not specified (implied missing). (b) Rewrite: 'Subject: Q2 Campaign Kickoff — need input by Friday Mar 21. Team — We need to finalize Q2 campaign direction by Friday so creative production can start March 24. Please reply by end of day Friday with your input on: (1) Primary channel mix (social/email/search/display), (2) Target audience segment priority, (3) Any capacity concerns for your function. I will consolidate responses and schedule a 30-minute decision meeting Monday Mar 24 at 10am to finalize and assign owners. If you cannot respond by Friday, please let me know so I can adjust the timeline. Thanks, Sarah' (c) The rewrite is more effective because it specifies the subject, deadline, required input (three specific questions), consequence of delay (creative production slips), and next step (meeting with time and purpose). It respects recipients' time by telling them exactly what they need to do and by when. It creates accountability without being harsh.

rubric.key: Identifies at least five specific problems
Explanation connects rewrite to communication principles

questions.question: Apply the writing process (plan, draft, revise, edit) to produce a 150-word executive summary of the following situation: 'Our e-commerce site's mobile conversion rate has declined from 2.8% to 1.9% over the past 90 days. Desktop conversion is unchanged at 4.1%. Investigation showed that a new checkout flow deployed 90 days ago added two additional steps on mobile but not on desktop, and user testing shows mobile shoppers abandoning at the new steps. Engineering estimates a rollback would take 2 weeks; a redesigned single-page mobile checkout would take 6 weeks but perform better than the original.' Executive summary should be written for the CEO.
questions.answer: Planning: audience is CEO (busy, decision-oriented, wants bottom line first); purpose is to inform and get approval for a path forward; key message is that a regression is causing lost revenue and there are two options. Draft/revise executive summary: 'Mobile conversion rate has fallen from 2.8% to 1.9% since the new checkout flow launched 90 days ago, representing an estimated $340K in lost monthly revenue. Root cause is the two additional steps that were added to mobile but not desktop; user testing confirms shoppers are abandoning at these new steps. Desktop conversion is unchanged at 4.1%, confirming the issue is mobile-specific. Two options: (1) Roll back mobile to the previous flow — 2 weeks to implement, restores the 2.8% baseline but does not address original friction that motivated the redesign. (2) Deploy a single-page mobile checkout — 6 weeks to implement, projected to exceed the previous 2.8% baseline based on competitor benchmarks. Recommendation: execute Option 1 immediately to stop bleeding while Option 2 is built in parallel. Estimated recovered revenue: $680K over the 6 weeks to full solution. Seeking your approval to proceed with both tracks.' [155 words, within range.] The structure leads with the impact (dollars), explains the cause, presents options with trade-offs, recommends a path, and explicitly asks for the decision.

rubric.key: Leads with the business impact, not process details

questions.question: Explain the distinction between the direct approach and the indirect approach in professional writing. For each, give (a) a one-sentence rule, (b) one situation where it is the correct choice, and (c) one situation where it is the wrong choice.
questions.answer: DIRECT APPROACH: (a) Rule — state the main message or conclusion first, then provide explanation and details. (b) Right choice: routine positive news or neutral information — approvals, updates, announcements, requests between colleagues of equal rank. Also appropriate for busy senior executives who want the conclusion first and details only if needed. (c) Wrong choice: delivering emotionally loaded bad news to someone who will be hurt by it; in many high-context cultures (Japan, Korea) for sensitive topics; when relationships are fragile. INDIRECT APPROACH: (a) Rule — provide context, reasoning, and explanation before delivering the main message or bad news. (b) Right choice: bad-news messages to customers, employees, or external parties where you want to soften the blow and demonstrate reasoning; in many high-context cultures; when delivering news that might damage the relationship if delivered bluntly. (c) Wrong choice: busy executives who want the bottom line first; simple positive news where indirectness just wastes time; situations where stalling the bad news feels manipulative or evasive. The general rule: direct approach is more efficient, indirect approach is more diplomatic; match the approach to the audience, message, and cultural context.

questions.question: A marketing analyst is preparing a formal analytical report recommending that the company invest $500,000 in a new CRM system. Identify the standard sections of an analytical report and explain what each section should contain in this specific case.
questions.answer: Standard sections of an analytical report, with specific content for this case: (1) TITLE PAGE — title ('Proposal: Implementation of Salesforce CRM'), author, date, audience. (2) EXECUTIVE SUMMARY — the entire recommendation in 1-2 pages: the problem (lack of unified customer data across sales/support), the recommendation ($500K Salesforce implementation), the expected benefits (e.g., 15% revenue lift through better lead management, 20% support efficiency gain), and the decision requested. (3) INTRODUCTION — background, problem statement, scope and methodology; describes how the analyst collected data (current system audit, vendor research, ROI modeling). (4) FINDINGS/ANALYSIS — the substantive body: current state of customer data management, limitations causing revenue loss, criteria for evaluating alternatives, assessment of top CRM vendors (Salesforce, HubSpot, Microsoft Dynamics) against criteria, financial analysis (implementation cost, ongoing license cost, expected revenue lift, payback period). (5) CONCLUSIONS — what the findings mean, drawing them together into a coherent argument for the recommendation. (6) RECOMMENDATIONS — specific action: proceed with Salesforce implementation, project plan, timeline, budget, ownership. (7) APPENDICES — detailed vendor comparisons, financial model spreadsheets, reference customer interviews, implementation plans. Each section serves a purpose: executive summary for busy readers, analysis for skeptical reviewers who want to verify the reasoning, appendices for anyone who wants to dig deeper.

rubric.key: Specific content for the CRM case in each section
Explains the purpose each section serves

questions.question: Using Cialdini's six principles of influence (reciprocity, commitment and consistency, social proof, authority, liking, scarcity), construct a persuasive message asking a busy executive to agree to a 30-minute meeting to discuss a new project. Use at least three principles explicitly.
questions.answer: Sample persuasive message (using reciprocity, social proof, scarcity, and authority): 'Subject: 30 minutes to discuss Project Horizon — specific ask. Dear Amanda, Last quarter I shared the competitive analysis on our mobile market position that informed your board deck — I hope it was useful [RECIPROCITY: reminds her of past value delivered]. I am now working on Project Horizon, a proposal for how we respond to the new entrant that just raised $200M. The Product Leadership Council already reviewed an early draft and asked me to bring it to you before the April strategy review [SOCIAL PROOF: peers have engaged; AUTHORITY: legitimate process]. Could I request 30 minutes of your time next week? I have prepared a 5-page pre-read that will let us use the full 30 minutes for discussion rather than introduction. The time-sensitive element is that the April 15 board offsite will include strategic decisions on this topic, so I would like to get your input by April 10 [SCARCITY: deadline]. I have placeholder times at 10am Tuesday or 3pm Thursday — please let me know which works, or propose an alternative. Thank you, David.' This message uses reciprocity (past value), social proof (PLC engagement), authority (legitimate review process), and scarcity (April deadline). It is specific about the ask, respectful of her time, and demonstrates preparation.

rubric.key: At least three Cialdini principles applied

questions.question: Explain why the AIDA framework (Attention, Interest, Desire, Action) works for persuasive messages, using a specific example.
questions.answer: AIDA works because it matches the psychological stages a reader moves through when persuaded: first they need to pay attention, then they need their interest captured, then they need to want the thing, then they need to be told what to do. Skipping any stage breaks the persuasion. Example — a fundraising email from a nonprofit: ATTENTION: Opening line or subject that stops the reader — 'A 12-year-old girl in rural Kenya walks 3 hours for clean water — every morning.' Specific and concrete, unusual, creates immediate curiosity. INTEREST: Explain why this matters to the reader — 'Her name is Grace, and she misses the first half of school because of that walk. She wants to be a doctor. Clean water means school. School means possibility.' Ties the story to broader meaning. DESIRE: Help the reader picture the impact — '$50 builds a well that serves 40 families. Grace's village needs four wells. Our organization has built 2,300 wells in 15 years, each one tracked with GPS and photographed every six months so donors can see where their money went.' Makes the reader want the outcome. ACTION: Tell them exactly what to do — 'Donate today. Click this button. Any amount helps. If 100 people give $50, we reach Grace's village this year.' Specific, immediate, low-friction. Skipping stages fails: a message with only ACTION ('Donate to our nonprofit') doesn't motivate; a message with only ATTENTION and INTEREST but no ACTION leaves readers emotional but passive. The stages must all be present.

rubric.key: Accurate description of each AIDA stage
Concrete example applied to each stage

questions.question: A retail store manager needs to email a loyal customer that her special-order designer handbag (ordered 6 weeks ago, partial payment made) has been permanently discontinued by the manufacturer and cannot be delivered. She must offer a refund or alternative. Write a bad-news message using the indirect approach.
questions.answer: BAD NEWS EMAIL (indirect approach): 'Subject: Update on your Gucci Marmont order #18472. Dear Ms. Chen, Thank you for your patience while we have worked to fulfill your special order for the Gucci Marmont handbag in terracotta (Order #18472, placed February 10). We know how much you have been looking forward to it. [BUFFER: appreciative opening without false hope] We have been in close contact with the Gucci distribution team, and we learned yesterday that this particular style and color combination has been permanently discontinued by the manufacturer. Despite our efforts to locate remaining inventory through our partner stores, we have been unable to source one. [EXPLANATION: before the bad news; shows the effort] Unfortunately, this means we cannot fulfill your order as placed. [BAD NEWS: clearly stated, no evasion, but after context] We want to make this right. You have two options: (1) A full refund of your $540 deposit, processed today and appearing on your card within 3-5 business days. (2) An exchange for the Gucci Marmont in a different color (black, white, or beige are currently available at the same price point), or any other bag in our current collection. I would be happy to send you photos and arrange a private viewing if you prefer. [ALTERNATIVES: specific, concrete, not token] As a loyal customer for over four years, we value your business greatly, and we apologize for this disappointment. Please let me know which option works for you, or call me directly at 555-1234 if you would like to discuss. [CLOSE: personal, warm, call to action] Warm regards, Maria Gonzalez, Store Manager, Beverly Hills.' This message uses the indirect approach: buffer first (appreciation), explanation before bad news (shows effort), clear statement of the bad news, immediate alternatives, and a warm close that maintains the relationship.

rubric.key: Buffer opening that avoids false hope
Clearly stated bad news without evasion

questions.question: Compare a text-heavy bullet-point slide with an image-led slide for the same content: 'Our Q3 revenue grew 18% year-over-year to $42M, driven primarily by the enterprise segment which grew 34% to $28M, while consumer flat-lined at $14M.' For each version, describe what the slide looks like and evaluate it using Presentation Zen principles.
questions.answer: TEXT-HEAVY BULLET SLIDE (bad): Title: 'Q3 Revenue Results.' Body: five bullets reading 'Total Q3 revenue: $42M,' 'Year-over-year growth: 18%,' 'Enterprise segment: $28M (34% growth),' 'Consumer segment: $14M (flat),' 'Enterprise driving overall growth.' EVALUATION: Violates every Presentation Zen principle. (1) The slide is a wall of text the audience reads instead of listening to the speaker. (2) All data points have equal visual weight, hiding the key message (enterprise growth). (3) The slide tells the audience what the speaker is saying, making the speaker redundant. (4) Nothing is visually memorable or emotional. (5) The slide could be handed out as a document with no narrator and work just as well — which means the speaker is not adding value. IMAGE-LED SLIDE (good): Title: 'Enterprise is driving our growth.' Body: a large bar chart showing Enterprise at $28M (tall, bright color, labeled '+34%') next to Consumer at $14M (shorter, muted color, labeled 'flat'). No bullet points. No other text. EVALUATION: Follows Presentation Zen principles. (1) The chart is the message: enterprise is tall and growing, consumer is flat. The visual impression is instant and memorable. (2) The title explicitly states the takeaway rather than just naming the topic — Guy Kawasaki calls this 'slide as headline.' (3) The speaker's voice carries the numbers and context; the slide carries the memorable visual. (4) The slide would be incomprehensible as a handout because it depends on the speaker, which is exactly right — a slide deck is not a document. Principle: 'If it's readable as a document, it's a document. A presentation slide should support speech, not replace it.' Reynolds and Duarte have both written that the single biggest improvement most presentations need is to cut text by 80% and replace it with a single image or chart that makes one memorable point.

rubric.key: Applies Presentation Zen principles to evaluation
Identifies key distinction: slide supports speech vs replaces it

questions.question: What are three specific delivery techniques that improve presentation effectiveness, and why does each work?
questions.answer: (1) DELIBERATE PAUSES: After a key point, pause for 2-3 seconds before continuing. Why it works: the pause lets the audience absorb the point, creates emphasis, signals confidence, and prevents the speaker from rushing through important material. Nervous speakers fill every moment with sound, which dilutes their message. Confident speakers use silence as punctuation. Steve Jobs famously paused for several seconds after announcing the iPhone in 2007, letting the audience respond. (2) VOICE VARIETY (PACE, VOLUME, PITCH): Vary your speed, loudness, and tone instead of delivering in a monotone. Why it works: monotone delivery exhausts listeners regardless of content quality; varied voice creates emotional texture that holds attention and signals which points are important. Speed up through familiar material, slow down through critical points, drop volume for intimate moments, raise for energy. This is a learned skill that improves with deliberate practice. (3) EYE CONTACT WITH INDIVIDUALS (NOT SCANNING): Look at one audience member for 2-3 seconds, complete a thought, then shift to another. Why it works: direct eye contact with a single person creates the feeling of a conversation rather than a monologue; scanning the room rapidly signals nervousness and prevents connection with anyone. For large audiences, mentally divide the room into quadrants and engage each quadrant in turn. Each of these techniques is small individually but compounds: a speaker who uses deliberate pauses, varied voice, and real eye contact feels more competent and engaging than one who delivers identical content without them.

rubric.key: Explanation of why each technique works

questions.question: Identify three forms of 'chart junk' (Tufte's term) and explain why each damages communication. Then describe the principles of a well-designed chart.
questions.answer: CHART JUNK EXAMPLES: (1) Three-dimensional effects on 2D charts — adding 3D shadows or extrusion to bar charts or pie charts. Why it's bad: distorts perception of relative sizes (the bar closest to the viewer appears larger than it should), adds no information, and is aesthetically jarring. 2D charts communicate the data accurately; 3D 'enhancements' reduce accuracy for decoration. (2) Excessive gridlines, borders, and background colors — dark gridlines, heavy borders around every element, colored backgrounds, textured fills. Why it's bad: the ink-to-data ratio should favor data; every non-data mark competes with the data for the reader's attention. Tufte's rule: remove anything that does not directly communicate information. (3) Unnecessary legends and labels — lengthy legends, redundant axis titles, data labels on every single point. Why it's bad: forces the eye to scan back and forth between the chart and legend, interrupting understanding. Direct labels on the data (next to the line or bar) eliminate the legend. Too many data labels create clutter. PRINCIPLES OF WELL-DESIGNED CHARTS: (1) Data-ink ratio: maximize ink devoted to data, minimize decorative ink. (2) Chart type matches data: bars for comparison, lines for trends over time, scatter for correlation, stacked bars for composition. Using the wrong chart type (like a pie chart with 12 slices) obscures the data. (3) Accurate scales: start bar charts at zero; do not truncate y-axis to exaggerate differences; use consistent scales when comparing multiple charts. (4) Direct labeling when possible: put labels directly on the data rather than in a separate legend. (5) Clear title stating the finding, not just the topic: 'Enterprise revenue doubled in 3 years' is stronger than 'Enterprise Revenue Trends.' (6) Limited color palette: use color to distinguish categories, not for decoration; color-blind-accessible palettes; highlight the key point with saturation and mute the rest. Edward Tufte's books (The Visual Display of Quantitative Information) set the standard; Stephen Few's Show Me the Numbers extends the principles to business dashboards.

rubric.key: Three forms of chart junk with explanations

questions.question: A French manager and an Indonesian manager are collaborating on a joint project. The French manager gives direct negative feedback in the team meeting: 'This section of your analysis is wrong — here is why.' The Indonesian manager becomes distant and responsive. Diagnose using Erin Meyer's Culture Map framework, and recommend how the French manager should adapt.
questions.answer: DIAGNOSIS: Erin Meyer's Culture Map identifies eight scales; this incident primarily involves the EVALUATING scale (direct vs indirect negative feedback) and possibly also COMMUNICATING (low-context vs high-context). FRENCH culture sits toward the direct end of the Evaluating scale — direct negative feedback delivered openly is normal and can even be a sign of respect for the colleague's ability to hear critique. Criticism in front of peers is not automatically humiliating; it is information. INDONESIAN culture sits at the indirect end — negative feedback should be delivered privately, wrapped in positive context, softened with qualifiers, and never in front of peers. Public direct criticism is a deeply humiliating loss of face (malu) and can permanently damage the relationship. The French manager is not trying to humiliate; he is operating by his own cultural norms. The Indonesian manager is not being thin-skinned; he is reacting to what registers as a serious breach of respect. Both parties see their own behavior as professional and normal. REMEDIATION for the French manager: (1) Never give direct negative feedback to an Indonesian colleague in front of peers. Pull him aside privately or send a message in writing that he can process alone. (2) Use indirect phrasing that softens the critique. Instead of 'This section is wrong,' say 'I wonder if we might look again at this section together — I think there may be another angle worth considering.' This preserves face and opens a dialogue. (3) Begin with positive acknowledgment. 'Your analysis has good structure and addresses most of the points. One area I would like us to look at more carefully is...' (4) Consider using a more senior intermediary for particularly difficult messages, as this is culturally normal in Indonesia. (5) Have an explicit conversation about working styles early in the collaboration: 'In France we are comfortable with direct feedback. How do you prefer to exchange critique?' This meta-conversation reduces future misunderstandings. Neither side needs to abandon their identity — just adapt the style at cultural interfaces. This is what Meyer calls 'code-switching.'

rubric.key: Correct diagnosis using the Evaluating scale

questions.question: A manager schedules a 'status update' meeting every Monday morning with her team of seven engineers. Each engineer describes what they are working on for 5 minutes. The meeting takes 45 minutes. Engineers have started complaining that the meeting is 'a waste of time.' Diagnose the problem and propose two alternatives.
questions.answer: DIAGNOSIS: The meeting is failing for several reasons that together signal it should not exist in its current form. (1) The content (individual status updates) is a one-to-one communication (engineer to manager) but is being broadcast one-to-many. Engineers do not need to hear each other's detailed status; they need the manager to know theirs. (2) The meeting format (round-robin narration) is one of the least engaging possible: the speaker is performing, everyone else is passively waiting their turn, and no discussion happens. (3) The information produced is ephemeral — it lives in participants' short-term memory and is forgotten by Tuesday. No documentation, no decisions, no action items. (4) It consumes 5.25 engineer-hours per week (7 people × 45 minutes) for information that could be delivered in 5 minutes of writing by each engineer. (5) It is a synchronous meeting blocking everyone's calendars at the same time, making Monday morning a low-productivity block. TWO ALTERNATIVES: ALTERNATIVE 1 — WRITTEN ASYNC UPDATES: Each engineer posts a 5-line status update in a shared Slack channel or Notion document every Monday by 9am. Format: 'Last week: [what I did]. This week: [what I'm doing]. Blockers: [anything I need help with].' The manager reads them by 9:30 and responds in-thread to anyone with blockers. No meeting required. Time saved: ~5 hours per week for the team. The manager gets the same information she was getting before, in a more reviewable format, and engineers reclaim their morning. ALTERNATIVE 2 — 15-MINUTE STANDUP WITH STRICT RULES: If synchronous is desired for team cohesion, convert to a daily or Monday-only 15-minute standup meeting with strict rules: each person has 90 seconds, answers only three questions (yesterday, today, blockers), no discussion during the round, flag anything needing conversation for after the standup. This is the agile scrum format. Time per week: 15 minutes vs 45 minutes. RECOMMENDATION: Start with Alternative 1 and see if the manager and team miss the synchronous element. Most teams find they do not.

rubric.key: Identifies multiple problems with current meeting

questions.question: Compare the Johnson & Johnson Tylenol 1982 crisis response with the Better.com 2021 Zoom layoff. For each, identify (a) what the company communicated, (b) what it communicated poorly or well, and (c) what the lesson is for future crisis communicators.
questions.answer: JOHNSON & JOHNSON TYLENOL 1982: (a) WHAT WAS COMMUNICATED — Immediate acknowledgment of the deaths, a full nationwide recall of all 31 million bottles ($100M cost), daily press briefings led by CEO James Burke, cooperation with FDA and FBI, concrete actions (tamper-evident packaging industry-wide standard), and consistent messaging across all channels. (b) DONE WELL — Speed (response within hours, not days), transparency (full access to media and regulators), scale of action (full recall signaled the company valued customer safety above profit), sustained leadership presence (Burke personally visible throughout), and concrete remediation (new packaging delivered on industry). (c) LESSON — Over-investing in the initial response pays off in reputation long-term. The $100M recall was criticized by analysts as overreaction at the time but saved the Tylenol brand and cemented J&J's reputation for integrity for decades. The Tylenol case is studied in every business school and remains the gold standard. BETTER.COM 2021: (a) WHAT WAS COMMUNICATED — CEO Vishal Garg fired 900 employees (9% of staff) in a 3-minute Zoom call opening with 'If you are on this call, you are part of the unlucky group being laid off. Your employment here is terminated effective immediately.' In a separate internal memo later leaked, Garg called the fired employees 'lazy.' The video was on TikTok within hours. (b) DONE POORLY — Everything. Channel choice (mass Zoom instead of individual conversations), tone (detached, administrative, no acknowledgment of harm), lack of empathy (no words of appreciation, no acknowledgment of contributions, no offer of support), the subsequent 'lazy' characterization (insult on top of injury), failure to anticipate leaks, and the CEO's personal ownership of the decision without any preparation. (c) LESSON — Bad news delivered without empathy, at scale, via impersonal channel, by a visibly contemptuous leader creates permanent reputation damage. The specific act (layoff) was legal and might have been necessary; the communication choice was the disaster. Better.com's already-delayed IPO was further postponed, the CEO faced investor calls for resignation, and the company's recruiting pipeline collapsed. The lesson: the decisions to lay off are business decisions, but HOW you lay off is a leadership and communication decision, and it is where reputations are lost or preserved. GENERAL PRINCIPLE: In crisis communication, the speed, tone, channel, and empathy of the response matter as much as the underlying facts. A bad situation handled well can strengthen trust; a good situation handled badly can destroy it.

rubric.key: Correct identification of what was done well/poorly

questions.question: A project manager needs to tell a long-tenured employee that her assignment to a high-profile project is being rescinded because the project sponsor wants someone with more domain experience. The employee is well-liked, has been a reliable performer, and has been looking forward to this project for months. Walk through how you would apply the Difficult Conversations framework (Stone, Patton, Heen) to this conversation.
questions.answer: The Difficult Conversations framework identifies three simultaneous conversations: the WHAT HAPPENED conversation (facts and contribution), the FEELINGS conversation (emotions), and the IDENTITY conversation (what this says about who I am). All three must be addressed. PREPARATION (before the conversation): (1) Separate intent from impact. Your intent is not to hurt her; the impact will nonetheless be hurt. Anticipate and prepare for the impact, don't just focus on intent. (2) Shift from blame to contribution. You are not saying she is inadequate; you are describing a mismatch between the role requirements and her current experience. (3) Clarify the purpose. Not to prove you are right, not to make her feel better artificially, but to share the decision, acknowledge its impact, and talk about what comes next. (4) Prepare to listen as much as to speak. STARTING THE CONVERSATION: Ask to meet privately, in person or video (never text or email for this). Begin with directness but not brutality: 'I need to share a difficult decision with you. I want to tell you directly because I respect your work and I know this will be disappointing.' This frames the conversation honestly. THE THREE CONVERSATIONS: (1) WHAT HAPPENED: Explain the facts without blaming her. 'The project sponsor has decided to bring in a consultant with 15 years of specific domain experience in insurance regulation, which is a gap the project has surfaced. The decision was hers, not mine, and I am communicating it.' Avoid euphemisms and avoid overstating your own advocacy. (2) FEELINGS: Acknowledge the emotional impact. 'I know how much you were looking forward to this project and how much work you have already put in. This is disappointing and I am sorry for the impact on you.' Do not rush past the feelings or pretend they don't exist. Give her space to react; expect some version of anger or hurt. Do not argue with the feeling. (3) IDENTITY: Name the identity concern that will likely be present. 'I want to be clear: this decision is not a statement about your capability or your value to the organization. You have been a reliable and skilled contributor, and I would not want you to doubt that. This is about a specific gap on a specific project, not about you as a professional.' Most reassurances about identity backfire if they sound defensive; this one is specific and anchored to her actual record. CLOSING: 'I know you need time to process this. I want to talk about what comes next when you're ready — there are other projects we should discuss. Take the rest of the day if you need it, and we can meet again tomorrow or Friday.' A SPECIFIC NEXT STEP that demonstrates continued investment in her, without rushing her emotionally.

rubric.key: Separation of intent and impact, blame and contribution

questions.question: In one paragraph, identify the single most important communication insight from this course that you will carry into your career, and explain why.
questions.answer: Sample full-credit answer: The most important insight from this course is that clear thinking and clear communication are the same skill — you cannot communicate clearly what you have not thought through carefully, and the discipline of writing or speaking forces the clarification of thinking itself. Every framework we studied — from the writing process to AIDA to Difficult Conversations to Presentation Zen — converges on this. Sloppy, vague communication is almost always a symptom of sloppy, vague thinking; the solution is not a communication trick but returning to the thinking. Conversely, when you are forced to communicate clearly to a specific audience with specific stakes, you often discover that your own understanding was less sharp than you thought, and the process of communicating improves your thinking. Jeff Bezos's insistence on six-page narrative memos at Amazon reflects this insight: 'the reason writing a good 4-page memo is harder than writing a 20-page PowerPoint is because the narrative structure of a good memo forces better thought and better understanding of what's more important than what' [Bezos, 2018 shareholder letter]. This insight changes how I will approach work: when I struggle to explain something clearly, I will not assume the audience is slow; I will assume my own understanding needs more work. When I am asked to write a proposal or give a presentation, I will not treat it as a communication task but as a thinking task, with the writing and speaking as by-products of clear thinking. Communication skills are cognitive skills. (Other defensible answers: the primacy of audience analysis; the power of the indirect approach for bad news; the principle that how you deliver bad news determines reputation; that psychological safety is the foundation of communication in teams; that direct and indirect cultures produce legitimate different norms that must be respected.)

rubric.key: Specific insight grounded in course material
Coherent reasoning about why it matters

major_assignments.title: Analytical Business Report and Executive Summary
major_assignments.description: Choose a real business problem facing a company (either your current employer, a local business, or a publicly-traded company). Write a formal analytical report recommending a course of action. The report must include a one-page executive summary that can stand alone, followed by supporting analysis. This assignment tests your ability to research, analyze, and communicate in the format busy executives actually use.
objectives: Apply the formal report structure (exec summary, introduction, findings, conclusions, recommendations)
Write an executive summary that stands alone as a decision-support document
Use appropriate visualizations to support the analysis
Produce a report that would be taken seriously by a working executive
requirements: 1-page executive summary that explicitly answers: what is the problem, what are the options, what is recommended, what is required
At least 3 charts or tables supporting the analysis
Footnoted sources (no Wikipedia as primary source)
deliverables: Separate one-page executive summary also submitted as PDF
major_assignments.grading_rubric: Graded on structure adherence, quality of analysis, writing clarity, executive summary effectiveness, and visual communication quality.

rubric.key: Executive summary can stand alone and lead to a decision

major_assignments.title: Persuasive Presentation with Video Recording
major_assignments.description: Design and deliver a 10-12 minute persuasive presentation on a topic of your choice. You will present in class or submit a high-quality recording. The presentation must apply Presentation Zen principles (minimal text slides, image-led design) and demonstrate effective delivery (voice variety, eye contact, pacing, confident body language). Peer feedback will be part of the grade.
objectives: Design presentation slides following Presentation Zen/Duarte principles
Structure the presentation using AIDA or comparable persuasive framework
Deliver with voice variety, deliberate pauses, and real eye contact
Integrate storytelling and data to produce a memorable case
requirements: Slides follow image-led design: no more than 6 words per slide on average
Specific persuasive ask (the audience should know what they are being asked to do)
At least one data visualization integrated into the argument
Recorded if not delivered in class (recording must include speaker on camera)
deliverables: Recording of delivery (if not in class)
major_assignments.grading_rubric: Graded on slide design quality, delivery effectiveness, structure, and persuasive impact. Peer feedback will count as 20% of the presentation grade.

rubric.key: Slide design follows Presentation Zen principles
Delivery: voice, pace, eye contact, confidence
Persuasive impact: does the audience want to act

major_assignments.title: Difficult Conversation Role Play and Reflection
major_assignments.description: Choose a realistic difficult conversation (delivering bad news to a direct report, negotiating with a vendor, confronting a peer about a performance issue, declining a senior leader's request, responding to a customer complaint). Write a detailed preparation plan using the Difficult Conversations framework, conduct the conversation as a role-play with a classmate (recorded), and write a reflection on what went well and what you would change.
objectives: Prepare for a difficult conversation using the three-conversation framework
Conduct a realistic role-play demonstrating skilled communication
Reflect critically on your own communication behavior
requirements: 3-page preparation plan (scenario description, goals, three-conversation analysis, planned opening)
Recorded 10-15 minute role-play with a classmate (partner switches roles for their scenario)
3-page reflection on the role-play (what worked, what didn't, what you would change, what you learned about yourself)
major_assignments.grading_rubric: Graded on quality of preparation, skill in the role-play, and depth of reflection.

rubric.key: Preparation plan is thorough and uses the framework

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes (weeks 3, 6, 9, 12, 15) covering writing process, reports, persuasion, bad news, presentations, visual communication, meetings, cross-cultural, crisis communication, and synthesis

class_participation.description: Active engagement in in-class writing exercises, role plays, peer feedback, and case discussions

analytical_report_with_exec_summary.description: Assignment 1: formal analytical report with one-page executive summary on a real business problem

persuasive_presentation.description: Assignment 2: 10-12 minute persuasive presentation with slides and delivery

difficult_conversation_role_play.description: Assignment 3: preparation plan, recorded role-play, and reflection for a difficult conversation

course_policies.attendance: Communication is a skill you develop through practice. Attendance is required because many class sessions involve in-class writing exercises, role plays, and peer feedback that cannot be made up. Two unexcused absences allowed; further absences reduce the participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Written assignments are due at the start of class on the due date. Late submissions are penalized 10% per calendar day up to three days, after which the assignment receives zero. Extensions may be granted for documented illness or emergency if requested in advance.
course_policies.collaboration: The analytical report and the persuasive presentation are individual assignments. The difficult conversation role play requires a partner; partners evaluate each other's performance but each student writes their own preparation plan and reflection. Peer feedback on presentations is encouraged throughout the semester.
course_policies.academic_integrity: The university's academic integrity policy applies. Use of generative AI (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) for drafting assistance, grammar checking, or brainstorming is permitted and encouraged as a tool, but any AI-generated content must be reviewed, revised, and owned by the student. Submitting AI output unchanged as your own work is plagiarism. When AI is used substantively, disclose the tool and the prompt used.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a laptop or tablet with word processing software, presentation software (PowerPoint, Keynote, or Google Slides), and a webcam for video assignments. Access to Zoom, Google Meet, or equivalent video conferencing is required for the role play assignment.
